01news
codfish/semantic-release-action GitHub Action has been compromised
stepsecurity.io · 2026-06-24 17:55 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSai-coding-assistant-hijackinganthropicbunclaude-codecodfish-semantic-release-actiongithub-actionsgithub-dead-drop-c2oven-sh-setup-bunrevokeanditgoeskaboomrussiasemantic-releasesigstoressh-lateral-movementtag-hijackingthebeautifulsandsoftime
96
  • └
    news
    Compromised GitHub action codfish/semantic-release-action steals CI/CD secrets
    aikido.dev · 2026-06-24 21:06 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
    TAGSaikidobuncodfish-semantic-release-actiongit-tag-hijackinggithubgithub-actionsimposter-commit-attackmiasmanpmoven-sh-setup-bunredhat-cloud-servicessemantic-releasethebeautifulsnadsoftime
    In plain words

    A widely used GitHub add-on called codfish/semantic-release-action was secretly changed so that projects using certain version labels would run the attacker’s code the next time their automated release process started. The affected projects are those that used the listed v2, v3, v4, or v5 labels, and they may have exposed private release keys such as GitHub or npm publishing tokens. This matters because those keys can let an attacker publish software or access project systems, turning one compromised tool into a risk for many software projects that depended on it.

    Summary
    The codfish/semantic-release-action GitHub Action was compromised after attackers force-pushed malicious commits and repointed major version tags, causing workflows using those tags to run credential-stealing code. Projects using affected tags should treat CI/CD secrets such as GITHUB_TOKEN and NPM_TOKEN as potentially exposed and rotate them immediately.

    On Jun 24, 2026, the codfish/semantic-release-action GitHub Action was compromised through an imposter commit attack. An attacker force-pushed two malicious commits into the repository and repointed sixteen tags to them, including the floating major version tags v2, v3, v4, and v5. Any workflow referencing the action by one of those tags will pull and run the attacker’s code on its next CI run.

    The action has been a standard way to wire semantic-release into GitHub Actions since 2019 and has over 100 GitHub stars. Workflows using it for automated releases almost always hold a GITHUB_TOKEN and frequently an NPM_TOKEN with publish access. The article says this is exactly the kind of access an attacker wants to land inside.

    The malicious commits changed action.yml from a Docker-based action into a composite action that first called a clean pinned version of codfish/semantic-release-action, then installed Bun through oven-sh/setup-bun and executed an obfuscated index.js payload. The payload was 781,580 bytes, had the SHA-256 hash 9f93d77d32833a515bc406c46da477142bb1ac2babeecb6aa42f98669a6db015, and contained the marker string thebeautifulsnadsoftime. The article links that marker to the Miasma credential stealing toolkit and lists affected tags, malicious commits, and confirmed clean versions.

    • Because GitHub Actions resolves tag references when a workflow runs, moving a tag can silently change the code executed by future CI runs without changing the workflow file itself.
    • The malicious action preserved normal release behavior by calling the clean action first, making successful workflow runs an unreliable indicator that the action was safe.
    • Leaving the original Dockerfile, entrypoint.js, and test file in the repository helped hide the compromise because the action had been changed to a composite action that no longer used those files.
    • The payload used GitHub public commit search as a dead drop channel, which the article notes avoids the need for traditional attacker infrastructure and may not trigger typical outbound network monitoring.
    • Pinning GitHub Actions to immutable commits rather than movable tags is strongly implied as a practical defense, since the attacker’s control came from repointing version tags.
    Key Points
    • 01On Jun 24, 2026, the codfish/semantic-release-action GitHub Action was compromised when an attacker force-pushed two malicious commits into the repository and repointed sixteen tags to them, including v2, v3, v4, and v5.
    • 02Any workflow referencing the compromised action by one of the affected tags will pull and run the attacker’s code on its next CI run.
    • 03The malicious commits changed action.yml from a Docker-based action into a composite action that still ran the legitimate codfish/semantic-release-action at a clean pinned commit, then installed Bun and executed an obfuscated index.js payload.
    • 04The injected index.js payload was 781,580 bytes of obfuscated JavaScript and contained the dead drop marker string thebeautifulsnadsoftime, which the article links to the Miasma credential stealing toolkit.
    • 05The article lists affected tags, malicious commit hashes, the SHA-256 hash of the malicious index.js file, and notes that codfish/semantic-release-action versions v1.0.0 through v1.10.0 and v2.0.0 are confirmed clean.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This is a specific, high-impact software supply-chain compromise of a GitHub Action used in CI/CD release workflows, with affected tags causing future runs to execute credential-stealing code. It is urgent for security professionals because exposed GITHUB_TOKEN and NPM_TOKEN secrets could enable further repository or package-publishing abuse.

In plain words

An attacker changed the code behind codfish/semantic-release-action, a tool many GitHub projects use to automate software releases, so that projects using certain version labels ran the attacker’s hidden program after June 24, 2026. The affected people are open-source and company projects whose GitHub workflows used those labels, and potentially developers who later opened poisoned repositories on their own computers. This matters because the hidden program tries to steal access keys, use GitHub as a hiding place for stolen data and commands, and spread into other repositories, computers, and software packages.

Summary
The GitHub Action codfish/semantic-release-action was compromised via force-moved version tags, causing workflows using those tags to execute an obfuscated Bun-based payload that steals tokens, abuses GitHub APIs for C2/exfiltration, and attempts to spread through repositories, developer machines, SSH hosts, and package ecosystems. Users should assume any workflow run using affected tags after June 24, 2026 15:39 UTC may be compromised and rotate credentials/investigate immediately.

On June 24, 2026 at 15:39:06 UTC, an attacker force-pushed a malicious commit to codfish/semantic-release-action and redirected several version tags to point at it. Any GitHub Actions workflow that ran against the affected tags after that time executed the attacker’s payload inside the runner. The action is used to wrap semantic-release, which automates software versioning, changelogs, tagging, and publishing.

The attacker changed the action from a Docker-based action into a composite action that installed Bun using oven-sh/setup-bun and ran an obfuscated index.js payload. Both injected steps used if: always(), so the payload ran even if earlier workflow steps failed or were skipped. The payload is described as stealing GitHub OIDC tokens, harvesting Personal Access Tokens that match known GitHub token patterns, encrypting collected data with AES-128-GCM, and attempting to spread a backdoor into other repositories accessible with stolen credentials.

The article says the payload used public GitHub commit messages as a dead-drop command-and-control channel, including mechanisms named RevokeAndItGoesKaboom and TheBeautifulSandsOfTime. It also targeted AI coding assistant configurations, attempted SSH-based lateral movement, and carried secondary-stage builders for npm, Python, and Ruby package ecosystems. StepSecurity reported that Harden-Runner observed outbound traffic only to GitHub API infrastructure and said its Compromised Actions Policy blocks workflows that reference the compromised action.

  • The attacker used Bun instead of Node.js because the article says Bun lacks the --require hook interception used by most Node.js security tooling.
  • The payload used public GitHub commit messages as a command channel, making its control traffic look like normal GitHub API activity rather than communication with a separate suspicious server.
  • The malware attempted to hijack AI coding assistant configurations, including Claude, Codex, Gemini, Copilot, and others, so poisoned repositories could trigger execution later on developer machines.
  • The payload included a Russian locale killswitch that halted execution when the system locale or locale environment variables started with ru.
  • The campaign reused fingerprints from previously documented compromises of @antv/graphlib, echarts-for-react, and tanstack-react-router, including the RevokeAndItGoesKaboom and TheBeautifulSandsOfTime dead-drop identifiers.
Key Points
  • 01On June 24, 2026 at 15:39:06 UTC, an attacker force-pushed a malicious commit to codfish/semantic-release-action and redirected several version tags to point at that commit.
  • 02Workflows that ran codfish/semantic-release-action using affected tags after the tag changes executed a Bun-based, heavily obfuscated JavaScript payload inside the GitHub Actions runner.
  • 03The payload steals GitHub OIDC tokens, searches for Personal Access Tokens matching known GitHub token patterns, encrypts collected material with AES-128-GCM, and attempts to propagate a backdoor into other repositories accessible with stolen credentials.
  • 04The attacker changed the action from a Docker-based runner to a composite action that installs Bun with oven-sh/setup-bun and runs index.js, with both injected steps guarded by if: always().
  • 05The article states that StepSecurity added codfish/semantic-release-action to its Compromised Actions Policy and that Harden-Runner observed the malware using GitHub API traffic for command-and-control and exfiltration.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a must-read because it reports a specific, recent supply-chain compromise of a GitHub Action used in release workflows, with malicious tags causing affected CI runs to execute token-stealing malware. The payload’s use of GitHub APIs for command-and-control, repository backdooring, AI assistant hijacking, SSH propagation, and multi-ecosystem package propagation makes it broadly relevant and high impact for security professionals.

02news
CISA Warns Critical Lantronix EDS5000 Flaw Is Being Actively Exploited
thehackernews.com · 2026-06-24 17:19 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSbelgiumbridge-breakcisacommand-injectioncve-2025-67038cve-2026-34908cve-2026-34909cve-2026-34910lantronixlantronix-eds5000path-traversalremote-code-executionubiquitiunifi-osunited-states
94
  • └
    advisory
    CISA warns of max severity Ubiquiti flaws exploited in attacks
    bleepingcomputer.com · 2026-06-24 14:35 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
    TAGSaccess-control-bypassbishop-foxcisacommand-injectioncve-2025-67038cve-2026-34908cve-2026-34909cve-2026-34910eds5000lantronixpath-traversalremote-code-executionubiquitiunifi-osunited-states
    In plain words

    CISA is warning that hackers are already using serious flaws in Ubiquiti UniFi OS devices and Lantronix EDS5000 servers. The affected organizations are anyone using those products, including U.S. federal agencies, which have been told to fix them within three days. These flaws matter because they could let an attacker change a system without permission, read sensitive files, steal credentials, or take full control of a device.

    Summary
    CISA says attackers are actively exploiting critical Ubiquiti UniFi OS and Lantronix EDS5000 flaws that can enable unauthorized changes, sensitive file access, or remote command execution. Administrators should apply the available patches or mitigations immediately, with U.S. federal agencies required to do so within three days.

    CISA is warning that hackers are actively exploiting flaws in Ubiquiti UniFi OS and Lantronix serial-to-ethernet servers. Under BOD 26-04, federal agencies have three days to apply available security updates or vendor-recommended mitigations. CISA added three Ubiquiti vulnerabilities and one Lantronix vulnerability to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog.

    The Ubiquiti flaws are CVE-2026-34908, CVE-2026-34909, and CVE-2026-34910. They involve an access control bypass, directory/path traversal, and improper input validation that can allow unauthorized system changes, access to sensitive files, and arbitrary operating system command execution. Ubiquiti released security updates in May and warned that the flaws could be exploited remotely without privileges.

    Researchers at Bishop Fox demonstrated that the three Ubiquiti flaws could be chained to achieve full remote code execution with elevated privileges on vulnerable UniFi OS devices. The Lantronix issue, CVE-2025-67038, is a critical root-level command injection flaw affecting EDS5000 firmware 2.1.0.0R3, and Lantronix recommends upgrading to version 2.2.0.0R1. CISA has not shared details about the observed exploitation, and the ransomware-use flag is listed as unknown for all four flaws.

    • Organizations using UniFi OS should not treat the three Ubiquiti flaws as separate low-level issues, because Bishop Fox showed they can be chained into full remote code execution with elevated privileges.
    • The Ubiquiti fixes have been available since May, so current exposure may indicate devices that were not updated despite vendor patches already being released.
    • Defenders have a practical way to check for vulnerable UniFi OS instances because Bishop Fox released a free detection script on GitHub.
    • Lantronix EDS5000 users can narrow their immediate firmware check to systems running version 2.1.0.0R3 and should upgrade to 2.2.0.0R1 as recommended by the vendor.
    • CISA confirmed active exploitation but did not provide details about how the flaws are being used, and the ransomware-use status is listed as unknown for all four vulnerabilities.
    Key Points
    • 01CISA warned that hackers are actively exploiting flaws in Ubiquiti UniFi OS and Lantronix serial-to-ethernet servers.
    • 02Under BOD 26-04, federal agencies have three days to apply available security updates or vendor-recommended mitigations for the listed vulnerabilities.
    • 03The Ubiquiti vulnerabilities CVE-2026-34908, CVE-2026-34909, and CVE-2026-34910 can allow unauthorized system changes, sensitive file access, and arbitrary operating system command execution.
    • 04Bishop Fox demonstrated that the three Ubiquiti flaws could be chained to achieve full remote code execution with elevated privileges on vulnerable UniFi OS devices.
    • 05Lantronix fixed CVE-2025-67038 in EDS5000 firmware by recommending users upgrade to version 2.2.0.0R1.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This is a must-read because CISA reports active exploitation of multiple critical flaws affecting Ubiquiti UniFi OS and Lantronix EDS5000 devices. The article includes concrete patch guidance and a three-day remediation deadline for U.S. federal agencies, making it immediately actionable for security teams managing these products.

    Reports
    • Latest Firmware for the EDS5000 series EDS5008 EDS5016 EDS5032
      Lantronix
      The article says Lantronix released a patch for CVE-2025-67038 and recommends upgrading EDS5000 devices to version 2.2.0.0R1.
In plain words

CISA warned that attackers are actively taking advantage of a serious flaw in Lantronix EDS5000 devices, and told U.S. federal civilian agencies to install fixes by June 26, 2026. The flaw can let attackers run their own commands with high-level control, and CISA also confirmed similar active abuse of serious flaws in Ubiquiti UniFi OS. This matters because these devices can be central parts of networks, so a successful break-in could help attackers move further inside and affect more systems.

Summary
CISA says a critical Lantronix EDS5000 command-injection flaw is being actively exploited and ordered U.S. federal agencies to patch by June 26, 2026. The agency also confirmed active exploitation of three critical Ubiquiti UniFi OS flaws that can be chained for root-level remote code execution.

CISA warned that a critical security flaw affecting Lantronix EDS5000 Series devices is being actively exploited. The flaw, tracked as CVE-2025-67038, has a CVSS score of 9.8 and is described as a code injection issue. CISA urged Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to apply fixes by June 26, 2026.

The vulnerability exists in the HTTP RPC module, which executes a shell command to write logs when user authentication fails. The username is directly added to the command without sanitization, allowing attackers to inject arbitrary operating system commands through the username parameter. The injected commands are executed with root privileges.

CISA also confirmed active exploitation of three maximum-severity flaws in Ubiquiti UniFi OS: CVE-2026-34908, CVE-2026-34909, and CVE-2026-34910. The article says these flaws can be chained to obtain a reverse shell with full root privileges in a single request, according to Bishop Fox. Belgium’s Centre for Cybersecurity warned that compromise of UniFi OS devices could allow unauthorized changes, access to sensitive files, information disclosure, arbitrary command execution, lateral movement, and broader network compromise.

  • The Lantronix flaw is especially actionable because exploitation occurs through the username parameter during failed authentication, meaning fixes should be prioritized even if attackers do not have valid credentials.
  • CISA gave Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies only until June 26, 2026, to remediate the Lantronix flaw, signaling high urgency after adding it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog.
  • The article notes there are no public details on how CVE-2025-67038 is being exploited or who is behind the activity, so defenders cannot rely on attacker-specific indicators alone.
  • The UniFi OS vulnerabilities have already been chained in a public proof-of-concept to obtain root privileges in a single request, increasing the practical risk for unpatched systems.
  • Because UniFi OS devices are often centrally integrated into networks, the article highlights that compromise could support lateral movement and broader network compromise beyond the affected device itself.
Key Points
  • 01CISA warned that CVE-2025-67038, a critical Lantronix EDS5000 Series code injection flaw with a CVSS score of 9.8, is being actively exploited.
  • 02CVE-2025-67038 allows attackers to inject arbitrary operating system commands into the username parameter, and the injected commands are executed with root privileges.
  • 03CISA urged Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to apply fixes for the Lantronix EDS5000 flaw by June 26, 2026.
  • 04CISA also confirmed active exploitation of three maximum-severity Ubiquiti UniFi OS flaws tracked as CVE-2026-34908, CVE-2026-34909, and CVE-2026-34910.
  • 05Bishop Fox detailed a proof-of-concept that chains the three UniFi OS flaws to obtain a reverse shell with full root privileges in a single request, and Ubiquiti released patches late last month.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a must-read because it reports active exploitation of a critical Lantronix EDS5000 flaw with root-level command execution impact and a CISA remediation deadline for U.S. federal agencies. It also notes active exploitation of three maximum-severity Ubiquiti UniFi OS flaws that can be chained for root-level remote code execution, increasing urgency for defenders of affected environments.

Reports
  • CISA Adds Four Known Exploited Vulnerabilities to Catalog
    CISA
    The article cites this CISA alert as the source for the warning that the Lantronix EDS5000 flaw is being actively exploited.
  • Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog
    CISA
    The article references CISA's catalog for the remediation deadline requiring Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to apply fixes.
  • Latest Firmware for the EDS5000 series EDS5008 EDS5016 EDS5032
    Lantronix
    The article links to Lantronix firmware information while describing the CVE-2025-67038 code injection flaw and available fixes.
  • Popping Root on UniFi OS Server: Unauthenticated RCE Chain Detection & Analysis
    Bishop Fox
    The article references Bishop Fox's analysis of a proof-of-concept chain that obtains a root reverse shell on UniFi OS in a single request.
  • Security Advisory Bulletin 064-064
    Ubiquiti
    The article cites Ubiquiti's advisory as the source for patches released for the three UniFi OS flaws.
  • Warning: Ubiquiti has addressed multiple critical vulnerabilities in UniFi OS - patch now
    Belgium Centre for Cybersecurity
    The article quotes this advisory to explain the potential impact of the UniFi OS vulnerabilities on affected devices and networks.
03news
Mandiant reveals how Cisco SD-WAN zero-day attacks gained root access
bleepingcomputer.com · 2026-06-24 21:29 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSanti-forensicsciscocisco-catalyst-sd-wan-managercommand-injectioncve-2026-20127cve-2026-20182cve-2026-20245malicious-csv-uploadmandiantprivilege-escalationrogue-sd-wan-peeringtrootvbondvmanagevsmart
94
In plain words

Hackers used a previously unknown flaw in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN systems, which are used to manage network connections, to create hidden accounts with full control on targeted devices. Mandiant says the attackers first got access to some devices, then uploaded a specially made file, changed passwords, collected network settings, and tried to erase evidence afterward. This matters because affected organizations, including a service provider mentioned in the report, could have had their network management systems controlled by outsiders and unauthorized changes pushed to other connected devices.

Summary
Mandiant revealed that attackers exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN zero-day CVE-2026-20245 after gaining device access, using a crafted CSV upload to create rogue root accounts and then erase traces. Cisco has issued fixes, and organizations should upgrade and check for unauthorized peering or other compromise indicators.

Mandiant revealed new details on how hackers exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN vulnerability CVE-2026-20245 in zero-day attacks to create rogue root accounts on targeted devices. The flaw is a high-severity command injection issue in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, Controller, and Validator that allows authenticated attackers with local access to execute arbitrary commands as root by uploading a crafted file. Cisco previously said the vulnerability had been exploited in a limited number of attacks, released security updates, and stated that no workarounds were available.

According to Mandiant, the intrusion began with unauthorized SD-WAN peering connections on a service provider’s infrastructure. Starting in March 2026, the threat actor established rogue peer connections and authenticated to affected SD-WAN Manager devices using the vmanage-admin account. The attackers changed the default admin account password, logged in to the SD-WAN Manager web interface, extracted configuration information, and later restored the admin account to its original password.

Mandiant said the attackers exploited CVE-2026-20245 through a tenant-upload feature in the SD-WAN command-line interface by uploading a malicious CSV file named evil_tenant.csv. The payload backed up system configuration files such as /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow before creating a root-level account named troot, which the attackers used to gain full control of the device. The attackers also used anti-forensic tactics, including restoring modified files, deleting payloads and temporary files, erasing evidence of the rogue root account, and running a validation script to confirm traces had been removed.

  • The attackers restored the default admin account to its original password after using it, indicating that unchanged credentials do not rule out compromise.
  • Some rogue peering activity occurred on systems not vulnerable to the previously disclosed authentication-bypass flaws, so organizations should investigate unauthorized peering even if they were not exposed to those specific CVEs.
  • Cisco told Mandiant the breach did not involve CVE-2026-20182 and that stolen certificates from a previous compromise may have been used to regain access to devices.
  • Mandiant observed a validation script used to confirm that traces of compromise had been removed, making diagnostic data collection and checks for unauthorized peering especially important.
Key Points
  • 01Mandiant revealed that hackers exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN vulnerability CVE-2026-20245 in zero-day attacks to create rogue root accounts on targeted devices.
  • 02CVE-2026-20245 is a high-severity command injection flaw in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, Controller, and Validator that allows authenticated attackers with local access to execute arbitrary commands as root by uploading a crafted file.
  • 03Mandiant said the intrusion began with unauthorized SD-WAN peering connections on a service provider's infrastructure, followed by authentication to affected SD-WAN Manager devices using the vmanage-admin account.
  • 04The attackers exploited CVE-2026-20245 through a tenant-upload feature in the SD-WAN command-line interface by uploading a malicious CSV file named evil_tenant.csv.
  • 05Mandiant reported that the attackers used anti-forensic tactics, including restoring modified configuration files, deleting the malicious payload and temporary files, and erasing evidence of the rogue root account.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a must-read because it describes active zero-day exploitation of Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN devices to gain root access and create rogue accounts. It includes concrete exploitation details, affected Cisco products, anti-forensic behavior, and recommended checks for organizations that may be impacted.

Reports
  • Zero-Day Exploitation of Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager
    Mandiant
    The article says Mandiant published a report revealing how CVE-2026-20245 was exploited as a privilege-escalation vulnerability after attackers had already gained access to targeted SD-WAN devices.
  • Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Privilege Escalation Vulnerability
    Cisco
    The article links to Cisco's security advisory for CVE-2026-20245 and summarizes Cisco's description of the flaw, affected products, exploitation conditions, and available fixes.
04news
Cisco Unified CM Flaw Exploited After PoC Reveals File-Write Path to Root
thehackernews.com · 2026-06-24 06:50 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSarbitrary-file-writecatalyst-sd-wan-managerciscocisco-unified-communications-managercisco-unified-communications-manager-session-management-editioncisco-webdialercve-2026-20230cve-2026-20262defused-cyberprivilege-escalationremote-code-executionssd-secure-disclosuressrf
94
In plain words

Attackers have started using a newly disclosed flaw in Cisco Unified Communications Manager and its Session Management Edition, products used to manage business communications. The issue can let someone on the internet send a specially made web request and write files onto the affected system, which could later help them gain full control. Organizations are mainly at risk if a feature called WebDialer is turned on, and Cisco has released fixed versions while advising users to turn that feature off if they cannot update right away.

Summary
Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical Cisco Unified CM flaw that can let unauthenticated attackers write files and potentially escalate to root when WebDialer is enabled. Cisco has patched the issue in Unified CM/SME 14SU6 and 15SU5, and organizations should patch or disable WebDialer immediately.

Threat actors have begun exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical security flaw affecting Cisco Unified Communications Manager and Unified Communications Manager Session Management Edition. Cisco described the issue as improper input validation for specific HTTP requests that could allow an unauthenticated remote attacker to conduct server-side request forgery attacks through an affected device. A successful exploit could allow the attacker to write files to the underlying operating system that could later be used to elevate to root.

Defused Cyber said it observed active exploitation of the vulnerability, with file-write payloads landing on its decoys from a single source using an unvetted proof of concept. Successful exploitation requires the WebDialer service to be enabled, although the article notes that WebDialer is disabled by default. Cisco provides steps to check whether the Cisco WebDialer Web Service is started in the Unified CM Administration interface.

Cisco patched the vulnerability in Unified CM and Unified CM SME versions 14SU6 and 15SU5. If immediate patching is not possible, the article says users are advised to disable the WebDialer service until a fix can be applied. SSD Secure Disclosure also published technical specifics describing the issue as a path to arbitrary file writing and code execution through the WebDialer component.

  • Exposure appears to depend on a non-default configuration, because the WebDialer service must be enabled even though it is disabled by default.
  • Defused Cyber reported exploitation from a single source using an unvetted proof of concept, suggesting observed activity may be tied to recently published technical details rather than a mature campaign described in the article.
  • Cisco had not yet updated its advisory to reflect active exploitation, so defenders relying only on the vendor advisory could miss the article’s reported exploitation status.
  • SSD Secure Disclosure’s write-up connects the WebDialer component to a path from unauthenticated file writing to code execution, making WebDialer status a high-priority check for affected deployments.
Key Points
  • 01Threat actors have begun exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical flaw affecting Cisco Unified Communications Manager and Unified Communications Manager Session Management Edition.
  • 02Cisco said the flaw is caused by improper input validation for specific HTTP requests and could allow an unauthenticated remote attacker to conduct server-side request forgery attacks through an affected device.
  • 03A successful exploit could allow an attacker to write files to the underlying operating system that could later be used to elevate to root.
  • 04Successful exploitation requires the Cisco WebDialer Web Service to be enabled, and Cisco said the service is disabled by default.
  • 05Cisco patched the vulnerability in Unified CM and Unified CM SME versions 14SU6 and 15SU5, and advised disabling WebDialer if immediate patching is not possible.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a must-read because it reports active exploitation in the wild of CVE-2026-20230 affecting Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME. The flaw can allow unauthenticated attackers to write files and potentially elevate to root when WebDialer is enabled, and Cisco has released patched versions and a mitigation.

Reports

    Cisco
    The article cites Cisco's advisory for details on the flaw, affected WebDialer configuration, checking whether the service is enabled, and patched versions.
  • Cisco Unified Communications Manager Arbitrary File Write to RCE
    SSD Secure Disclosure
    The article says SSD Secure Disclosure published additional technical specifics describing how the WebDialer component can be leveraged for arbitrary file writing and code execution.
05news
Malicious Edge extension abuses Native Messaging as bridge to malware
bleepingcomputer.com · 2026-06-24 20:58 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSautohotkeychrome-native-messagingedgecutionmicrosoft-365microsoft-edgemicrosoft-teamsoutlookpayouts-kingspowershellpythonzscaler
92
In plain words

Attackers used fake IT support messages on Microsoft Teams to trick employees into visiting a fake Microsoft update page, which installed a harmful Microsoft Edge add-on called Edgecution. That add-on could quietly start another program on the employee’s computer, letting the attackers run commands, collect system details, write files, and keep access. This matters because it shows ransomware-linked attackers finding ways to turn a normal browser feature into a path for taking over workplace computers.

Summary
A malicious Microsoft Edge extension called Edgecution is being used in ransomware-linked attacks to abuse Chrome Native Messaging, escape the browser sandbox, and launch a Python backdoor on infected Windows systems. The campaign starts with fake IT support messages on Microsoft Teams directing victims to a fraudulent Microsoft update page.

A malicious Microsoft Edge extension dubbed Edgecution has been used in a ransomware attack to escape the browser sandbox and deploy a Python-based backdoor. Zscaler researchers believe Edgecution is deployed by an initial access broker connected to the Payouts Kings ransomware operation. The compromise begins with an attacker posing as IT support on Microsoft Teams and directing employees to a fraudulent page under the pretense of installing a spam filter update.

The fake Microsoft update site presented download buttons for update packs or software verification, but those buttons downloaded malicious components, copied scripts to the clipboard, or launched forms requesting Microsoft 365 and Outlook passwords. The site offered multiple deployment options through an AutoHotKey script, a Windows batch script, and a PowerShell script. The malware components were delivered in a ZIP archive with malformed headers, which the scripts later fixed before extracting files and creating a scheduled task to run Microsoft Edge.

Edgecution runs in a headless Edge browser and uses Chrome Native Messaging to communicate with a local application outside the browser. The attack includes a malicious Edge extension disguised as an Edge Monitoring Agent and a Python-based backdoor that can execute shell commands, run PowerShell, run arbitrary Python code, write files, enumerate running processes, and gather system information. Zscaler said the technique shows evolving sophistication among ransomware-linked threat actors and recommended stronger monitoring of browser extensions and strict controls over native messaging host configurations.

  • The attack chain used malformed ZIP headers so security products would not recognize the downloaded file as a valid archive, while later scripts repaired the headers before extraction.
  • The fake Microsoft update page gave the attacker multiple deployment paths, including an AutoHotKey script, a Windows batch script, and a PowerShell script.
  • The malware ran Microsoft Edge in headless mode, making the malicious browser activity invisible to the user.
  • The ZIP archive included both an embedded Python 3.13.3 runtime and separate extension and native directories, showing that the attackers packaged the browser component and host-level executor together.
  • Zscaler found unused commands in both malware components, indicating that the analyzed version contains functionality that could be enabled in future versions.
Key Points
  • 01A malicious Microsoft Edge extension dubbed Edgecution has been used in a ransomware attack to escape the browser sandbox and deploy a Python-based backdoor.
  • 02The compromise begins with an attacker posing as IT support on Microsoft Teams and directing employees to a fraudulent page presented as a spam filter or Microsoft Outlook update site.
  • 03Zscaler researchers believe Edgecution is deployed by an initial access broker connected to the Payouts Kings ransomware operation.
  • 04Edgecution abuses Chrome Native Messaging to let the malicious Edge extension communicate with a local Python-based backdoor that can execute shell commands, run PowerShell, run arbitrary Python code, write files, enumerate processes, and gather system information.
  • 05Zscaler recommends that organizations strengthen monitoring of browser extensions and enforce strict controls over native messaging host configurations.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a high-impact, specific report of a ransomware-linked attack using a malicious Microsoft Edge extension to escape the browser sandbox and deploy a Python backdoor. Security professionals should read it because it describes an active technique abusing Chrome Native Messaging and includes concrete defensive recommendations and IoCs from Zscaler.

Reports
  • Payouts King Ransomware Initial Access Broker Deploys New Edgecution
    Zscaler
    The article relies on Zscaler's security research and technical analysis describing the Edgecution malware, its deployment methods, native messaging abuse, and indicators of compromise.
06news
Stealthy Mistic backdoor linked to ransomware access broker KongTuke
bleepingcomputer.com · 2026-06-24 10:41 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGS8baseakirablack-bastaclickfixcobalt-strikecrashfixfilefixinterlockkongtukemisticmodeloratmtlbackdoorqilinrhysidawoodgnat
92
In plain words

Researchers found a new hidden tool called Mistic being used in financially motivated break-ins against organizations in insurance, education, IT, and professional services. It is linked to KongTuke, a group that breaks into company networks and sells that access to ransomware gangs. This matters because the tool is designed to stay quietly inside a network for a long time, steal login details, move files, and help criminals prepare for bigger attacks.

Summary
A new stealthy backdoor called Mistic has been linked to KongTuke/Woodgnat, an initial access broker that sells corporate network access to ransomware groups. The malware is built for long-term persistence, in-memory execution, credential theft, and expanding attacker capabilities while minimizing detection.

A new backdoor called Mistic has been observed in financially motivated attacks against organizations in the insurance, education, IT, and professional services sectors. Symantec says the malware has been used in intrusions since April and believes it is linked to KongTuke/Woodgnat, an initial access broker active since at least 2024. KongTuke specializes in compromising corporate networks and selling access to ransomware groups including Qilin, Interlock, Rhysida, Akira, 8Base, and Black Basta.

In attacks investigated by Symantec, the infection began with the legitimate MpExtMs.exe executable side-loading a malicious DLL named version.dll, which loaded Mistic as EndpointDlp.dll. The filename resembles Microsoft endpoint security tooling, which researchers say may help the malware blend in with trusted software on the host. A separate .NET DLL also displayed a fake login screen to steal the victim’s account credentials.

Mistic can upload, download, move, rename, and delete files, create folders, change how often it checks in with its command-and-control server, execute code in memory, and delete itself from the host. Symantec says these features are consistent with long-term, low-visibility access, while Zscaler tracks the malware as MTLBackdoor and reported it as a payload in a multi-stage ClickFix infection chain in May. Zscaler also noted that Mistic can load Beacon Object Files, allowing it to expand its capabilities while leaving no footprint on disk.

  • Mistic’s filename, EndpointDlp.dll, appears chosen to resemble Microsoft endpoint security tooling, which may help it blend in with trusted software on compromised hosts.
  • The attack chain abuses the legitimate MpExtMs.exe executable to load a malicious version.dll, making monitoring for suspicious DLL side-loading around trusted executables especially relevant.
  • Mistic’s ability to run payloads only in memory and delete itself gives operators a way to reduce evidence left on disk during long-term access operations.
  • Zscaler’s finding that Mistic can load Beacon Object Files shows the backdoor can be extended with additional post-compromise capabilities without relying on separate files written to disk.
  • The appearance of Mistic shortly after ModeloRAT in at least one incident suggests KongTuke may use multiple custom backdoors in the same intrusion workflow.
Key Points
  • 01A new backdoor called Mistic has been observed in financially motivated attacks targeting organizations in the insurance, education, IT, and professional services sectors.
  • 02Symantec believes Mistic is linked to KongTuke/Woodgnat, an initial access broker that sells compromised corporate network access to ransomware groups including Qilin, Interlock, Rhysida, Akira, 8Base, and Black Basta.
  • 03In attacks investigated by Symantec, the infection chain used the legitimate MpExtMs.exe executable to side-load a malicious version.dll loader for Mistic, also named EndpointDlp.dll.
  • 04Mistic can upload, download, move, rename, and delete files, create folders, change its command-checking frequency, execute code in memory, and terminate itself while deleting files from the host.
  • 05Zscaler tracks Mistic as MTLBackdoor and reported that it was delivered as a payload in a multi-stage ClickFix infection chain in May.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a must-read because it reports active in-the-wild use of a new stealthy backdoor linked to KongTuke/Woodgnat, an access broker connected to multiple ransomware groups. The article includes concrete attack-chain details, capabilities, and related delivery methods that are directly relevant for detection and threat hunting.

Reports
  • New Mistic Backdoor / ModeloRAT research
    Symantec
    The article cites Symantec researchers’ analysis of Mistic’s stealth features, capabilities, attack chain, and suspected link to KongTuke/Woodgnat.
  • Technical Analysis of MTLBackdoor
    Zscaler
    The article describes this as a Zscaler technical report that tracks Mistic as MTLBackdoor and says it was delivered in a multi-stage ClickFix infection chain.

Amadey and StealC Malware Network Disrupted, 27M Stolen Credentials Recovered
thehackernews.com · 2026-06-24 15:59 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSamadeybelgiumcanadaclickfixdenmarkfrancegermanyincreasenetherlandsphishing-campaignsplymouthsocgholishstealcunited-kingdomunited-states
90
  • └
    news
    Three ‘cybercrime as a service’ operations undercut by Microsoft, law enforcement
    therecord.media · 2026-06-24 20:00 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
    TAGSamadeyeuropolevil-corpfake-browser-updatesmicrosoftrussiasocgholishstealc
    In plain words

    Microsoft and international police took over hundreds of internet domains and servers used by criminal groups to break into computers, steal login details, and help launch scams and ransom attacks. They also recovered about 27 million stolen usernames and passwords and found €41 million in criminal crypto assets. Everyday people and businesses were affected, including retailers whose websites were secretly infected and victims whose computers had been compromised. This matters because the operation aimed to disrupt the shared tools criminals use to turn one break-in into larger fraud, service disruption, and attacks on important public systems.

    Summary
    Microsoft and international law enforcement disrupted major cybercrime-as-a-service infrastructure used to spread SocGholish, Amadey and StealC, seizing hundreds of servers and domains and recovering millions of stolen credentials. The operation targeted the malware supply chain that enables ransomware, fraud and broader intrusions.

    Microsoft and international law enforcement announced a two-week operation to disrupt cybercrime-as-a-service infrastructure used to deploy ransomware, commit financial fraud and attack critical infrastructure. Europol said 326 servers and 142 domains were dismantled, 27 million stolen login credentials were reclaimed, and crypto assets of criminal origin worth €41 million were found. The operation targeted infrastructure tied to the distribution of SocGholish, Amadey and StealC malware.

    Microsoft said the case reflects an approach focused on the cyberattack supply chain rather than only individual services. Its researchers used artificial intelligence to find that Amadey and StealC depend on the same infrastructure, and Microsoft said taking both down at once would have an exponential effect. Amadey is primarily used to break in, while StealC steals passwords and other sensitive data.

    SocGholish was described as a tool that compromises systems by sending fake browser updates through compromised websites. Europol said it found 14,971 infected websites belonging to everyday retailers that were infected by the variant. Europol also said SocGholish is tied to Evil Corp, a Russian cybercrime gang linked to large-scale money laundering and ransomware activities.

    • Microsoft said its researchers used artificial intelligence to find that Amadey and StealC depended on the same infrastructure, enabling the two malware operations to be disrupted together.
    • The article highlights that disrupting multiple linked tools at once can have an exponential effect because Amadey is used to break in while StealC is used to steal passwords and other sensitive data after access is gained.
    • The operation reflects a shift from targeting individual criminal services to targeting the broader cybercrime supply chain that supports ransomware, fraud and public-service disruption.
    • SocGholish’s use of compromised retail websites to deliver fake browser updates shows that ordinary shopping sites can become part of the infrastructure used to compromise victims.
    Key Points
    • 01An international operation announced by Europol and Microsoft dismantled 326 servers and 142 domains used by cybercrime-as-a-service operations.
    • 02Investigators recovered about 27 million stolen login credentials and identified crypto assets of criminal origin valued at €41 million, or $47 million.
    • 03The operation targeted infrastructure used to distribute SocGholish, Amadey and StealC malware, which other criminals paid to use for further crimes.
    • 04Microsoft said the action focused on the cybercrime supply chain, disrupting coordinated tools used for ransomware, financial fraud and attacks on public services.
    • 05Europol said it found 14,971 everyday retail websites infected with a SocGholish variant, and it linked SocGholish to the Russian cybercrime gang Evil Corp.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This is a specific, high-impact international disruption of cybercrime-as-a-service infrastructure tied to SocGholish, Amadey, StealC and Evil Corp. It has broad relevance because the operation affected hundreds of servers and domains, millions of stolen credentials, and malware used to enable ransomware, fraud and attacks on public services.

    Reports
    • StealC and Amadey: Breaking down infostealers and the cybercrime services that deliver them
      Microsoft
      The article says Microsoft posted new research on Amadey and StealC alongside the disruption announcement.
  • └
    news
    Amadey, StealC malware operations disrupted in Operation Endgame action
    bleepingcomputer.com · 2026-06-24 14:35 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
    TAGSamadeycanadaclickfixdenmarkeuropolfakeupdatesfilefixgermanylockbitmicrosoftnetherlandssocgholishstealcunited-kingdomunited-states
    In plain words

    Microsoft, Europol, and partners in several countries disrupted servers and web addresses used by the Amadey and StealC criminal tools, which are used to break into computers and steal passwords, cryptocurrency wallets, and other sensitive information. Investigators recovered about 27 million stolen logins from more than 385,000 compromised systems and identified over €41 million linked to criminal activity. This matters because those stolen logins can be sold or reused by other criminals to break into organizations, steal data, demand ransom payments, or commit financial fraud.

    Summary
    Microsoft, Europol, and international partners disrupted Amadey and StealC malware infrastructure as part of Operation Endgame, taking down hundreds of servers/domains and recovering millions of stolen credentials. The action raises costs for cybercriminals using these malware-as-a-service tools for credential theft, initial access, and ransomware enablement.

    Microsoft, Europol, and international partners disrupted infrastructure used by the Amadey and StealC malware operations as part of Operation Endgame. The action involved authorities and private partners from multiple countries taking down, seizing, blocking, or sinkholing infrastructure tied to the malware families. Europol said the operation disrupted 326 servers and 142 domains, identified more than €41 million in cryptocurrency linked to criminal activity, and recovered about 27 million stolen credentials from over 385,000 compromised systems.

    The operation targeted cybercrime infrastructure used to gain initial access to systems, steal credentials, and ultimately deploy ransomware or conduct financial fraud. Amadey and StealC are sold through malware-as-a-service operations, where affiliates pay for access to malware builders, management panels, support, and infrastructure. Amadey is used to gain an initial foothold on victim devices and deploy additional malware, while StealC is used to steal credentials, cryptocurrency wallets, and other sensitive information.

    Microsoft said it identified more than 200 malicious command-and-control domains and IP addresses associated with Amadey and StealC and worked with partners to shut down the infrastructure through court orders, domain seizures, registrations, and provider notifications. The company said the two malware families were linked to more than 140,000 infected devices during the first two weeks of May 2026 alone. Other private partners, including ESET, Proofpoint, IBM X-Force, and Bitsight, contributed by identifying infrastructure, providing intelligence, and supporting malware analysis.

    • The operation targeted multiple parts of the cybercrime supply chain at once, including tools used for initial access, credential theft, ransomware enablement, and financial fraud.
    • Stolen credentials from StealC are not only used by the original operators but are also sold through underground marketplaces and initial-access brokers, allowing other threat actors to reuse them for later intrusions.
    • The article notes that infrastructure takedowns can be temporary when arrests are not made, because threat actors commonly rebuild and resume attacks after disruptions.
    • The scale of infections in a short period was significant, with Microsoft linking Amadey and StealC to more than 140,000 infected devices in just the first two weeks of May 2026.
    Key Points
    • 01Microsoft, Europol, and international partners disrupted infrastructure used by the Amadey and StealC malware operations as part of Operation Endgame.
    • 02Europol said the operation disrupted 326 servers and 142 domains, identified more than €41 million in cryptocurrency linked to criminal activity, and recovered about 27 million credentials stolen from over 385,000 compromised systems.
    • 03The coordinated action also targeted SocGholish, a malware loader that infects visitors through compromised websites serving fake browser update prompts.
    • 04Amadey is used to gain an initial foothold on victim devices and deploy additional malware, while StealC is used to steal credentials, cryptocurrency wallets, and other sensitive information.
    • 05Microsoft said Amadey and StealC were linked to more than 140,000 infected devices during the first two weeks of May 2026 alone.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This is a specific, high-impact law enforcement disruption of active Amadey and StealC malware infrastructure, with hundreds of servers/domains affected and millions of stolen credentials recovered. It is broadly relevant because these malware families support initial access, credential theft, ransomware deployment, and financial fraud, though the article does not report a new vulnerability or direct patch action.

    Reports
    • ESET takes part in Operation Endgame to disrupt Amadey, StealC
      ESET
      The article says ESET released a report on its role identifying and disrupting infrastructure used by Amadey and StealC.
    • StealC you later: Proofpoint and X-Force support Operation Endgame disruptions
      IBM X-Force and Proofpoint
      The article says Proofpoint and IBM X-Force contributed intelligence and malware analysis supporting the disruption.
    • Bitsight Aids Disruption Efforts on Amadey Malware and StealC Malware
      Bitsight
      The article says Bitsight assisted by identifying and analyzing infrastructure associated with Amadey and StealC.
In plain words

Police and private companies worked together to disrupt the networks behind Amadey and StealC, tools criminals use to break into computers, steal logins, and install more harmful programs. They took down hundreds of servers and web domains, restricted more than $47 million in criminal cryptocurrency, and recovered 27 million stolen login details. This matters because these tools had infected many computers around the world and helped enable financial fraud, ransomware, and attacks on important services.

Summary
International law enforcement and private-sector partners disrupted the Amadey and StealC malware-as-a-service networks, taking down hundreds of servers and domains while recovering 27 million stolen credentials. The operation targeted key infrastructure used to deliver infostealers, enable fraud, and support broader cybercrime operations.

A coordinated law enforcement operation with private sector partners including Bitdefender, Bitsight, ESET, and Microsoft disrupted criminal infrastructure powering Amadey and StealC. Europol said the goal was to disrupt the cybercriminal “assembly lines” used to launch ransomware, financial fraud, and attacks on critical infrastructure. The action recovered 27 million stolen login credentials and identified, flagged, and restricted more than $47 million in cryptocurrency assets of criminal origin.

The operation dismantled 326 servers and 142 domains, and Microsoft said it flagged 200 malicious Amadey and StealC command-and-control domains and IP addresses that have since been shut down. Microsoft also said Amadey and StealC used some of the same infrastructure and were linked to more than 140,000 infected computers globally in the first two weeks of May 2026. The company identified more than 18,000 victim computers and severed criminal control of those devices.

Amadey and StealC are advertised under a malware-as-a-service model, with Amadey acting as a loader and StealC stealing data such as credentials, session cookies, credit card data, browsing history, and extension data. Amadey has been active since October 2018, while StealC first surfaced in January 2023 and is sold by a threat actor using the moniker “plymouth.” The latest effort took place between June 15 and 19, 2026, as part of Operation Endgame and involved authorities from Belgium, Canada, Denmark, France, Germany, the Netherlands, the U.K., and the U.S.

  • Microsoft reported that Amadey and StealC used some of the same infrastructure, showing that separate malware services can be operationally connected within the same criminal ecosystem.
  • StealC’s subscription model allowed unlimited build generation, which ESET said made it cheaper and easier for affiliates to rotate command-and-control infrastructure and generate new samples compared with Amadey’s pay-per-rebuild model.
  • Security flaws in StealC’s own control panel exposed details about its malware-as-a-service operation, and IBM X-Force and Proofpoint said one flaw was likely exploited by an affiliate to steal data from other affiliates.
  • Both Amadey and StealC include locale-based behavior tied to countries such as Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus, with StealC terminating itself for some locales and Amadey skipping some stealing functions on certain hosts.
  • Amadey’s payload distribution grew from 66 samples in 2019 to a peak of 11,635 in 2025, indicating that the loader became far more widely used before the 2026 disruption.
Key Points
  • 01A coordinated law enforcement operation with private sector partners including Bitdefender, Bitsight, ESET, and Microsoft disrupted criminal infrastructure powering Amadey and StealC.
  • 02The two-week action recovered 27 million stolen login credentials, dismantled 326 servers and 142 domains, and identified, flagged, and restricted more than $47 million in cryptocurrency assets of criminal origin.
  • 03Amadey and StealC are advertised under a malware-as-a-service model, with Amadey functioning as a loader and StealC stealing sensitive data such as credentials, session cookies, credit card data, browsing history, and extension data.
  • 04Microsoft said Amadey and StealC were linked to more than 140,000 infected computers globally in the first two weeks of May 2026 and that it identified over 18,000 victim computers and severed criminal control of those devices.
  • 05The operation took place between June 15 and 19, 2026, as part of Operation Endgame and involved judicial authorities and law enforcement from Belgium, Canada, Denmark, France, Germany, the Netherlands, the U.K., and the U.S.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific, high-impact law enforcement disruption of major malware-as-a-service infrastructure tied to Amadey and StealC, with 27 million stolen credentials recovered and more than 140,000 infected computers linked to the activity. It is broadly relevant for security professionals tracking credential theft, loaders, infostealers, and cybercrime infrastructure, though it is less immediately urgent than an active exploitation or emergency patch event.

Reports
  • Amadey C2 Analysis
    Mitsui Bussan Secure Directions
    The article cites Mitsui Bussan Secure Directions for data on the daily number of active Amadey command-and-control servers and Amadey payload distribution trends.
  • ESET takes part in Operation Endgame to disrupt Amadey, StealC
    ESET
    The article quotes ESET researchers on the Amadey and StealC affiliate models and administration panels.
  • StealC you later: Proofpoint and X-Force support Operation Endgame disruptions
    IBM X-Force
    The article references IBM X-Force for findings about security flaws in the StealC command-and-control panel, including a directory traversal issue.
  • StealC You Later: Proofpoint and IBM X-Force Support Operation Endgame
    Proofpoint
    The article references Proofpoint for findings about security flaws in the StealC command-and-control panel and their possible exploitation by an affiliate.
  • StealC and Amadey: Breaking down infostealers and the cybercrime services that deliver them
    Microsoft
    The article cites Microsoft’s analysis of Amadey and StealC infrastructure, infections, victim computers, and disrupted command-and-control domains and IP addresses.
