01research
Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks
thehackernews.com · 2026-06-24 12:48 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSadk-samplesapache-dorisazure-sentinelcloudflare-workers-sdkcommand-injectioncordycepscredential-theftgithubgithub-actionsgooglemicrosoftnovee-securityprivilege-escalationpython-blackpython-software-foundation
88
In plain words

Researchers found a weakness in the automated systems that many software projects use to review and build code on GitHub. More than 300 important repositories were fully exploitable, including ones connected to Microsoft, Google, Apache, Cloudflare, and the Python Software Foundation, meaning even an unauthenticated user with a free account could run their own code, steal credentials, or gain control over project workflows. This matters because these open-source projects are part of the basic software plumbing used across the industry, so a compromise could spread into the wider software supply chain.

Summary
Researchers found a CI/CD workflow weakness dubbed Cordyceps that could let unauthenticated attackers hijack GitHub Actions workflows, steal credentials, and compromise more than 300 major open-source repositories. Affected projects included repositories tied to Microsoft, Google, Apache, Cloudflare, and the Python Software Foundation, with several vendors confirming or patching issues after disclosure.

Cybersecurity researchers at Novee Security flagged a CI/CD workflow weakness called Cordyceps that can allow attackers to hijack workflows and compromise open-source supply chains. The company said the flaw is exploitable by any unauthenticated user, with no organization membership or special privileges required. Its scan of about 30,000 high-impact repositories found more than 300 to be fully exploitable.

The issue stems from weak CI/CD configurations that give pull requests more permissions than they should have. Because untrusted pull requests can trigger privileged workflows, attackers may be able to run code, steal credentials, escalate privileges, or compromise the software supply chain. Novee said the vulnerability can hide from scanners because the individual workflow components may be working as designed, while the risk emerges from how untrusted data crosses a trust boundary.

Novee reported examples involving repositories associated with Microsoft, Google, Apache, Cloudflare, and the Python Software Foundation. The findings included cases where pull request comments, forked pull requests, or crafted branch names could run attacker-controlled code on CI systems or expose credentials and tokens. Following responsible disclosure, Microsoft and Google confirmed impact, while Cloudflare, Python, and Apache applied hardening or patches.

  • The weakness can be exploited by any unauthenticated user with a free GitHub account, meaning the barrier to abusing affected workflows is extremely low.
  • Novee said the issue can hide from scanners because each workflow component may function as designed, while the danger emerges from how untrusted pull request data crosses a trust boundary.
  • Some reported cases required only low-effort triggers, such as a single pull request comment or a crafted branch name, to run attacker-controlled code in major organizations’ CI environments.
  • The article highlights non-expiring or high-privilege automation credentials as a key risk, because stolen CI tokens or GitHub App keys could allow attackers to approve pull requests or gain repository control.
Key Points
  • 01Novee Security identified a CI/CD workflow weakness called Cordyceps that can allow unauthenticated attackers to hijack workflows and compromise open-source supply chains.
  • 02A scan of about 30,000 high-impact repositories found more than 300 to be fully exploitable, enabling attacker-controlled code execution, credential theft, and supply chain compromise.
  • 03The issue stems from weak CI/CD configurations that grant pull requests more permissions than they should have, allowing untrusted pull requests to trigger privileged workflows.
  • 04Novee reported examples affecting repositories associated with Microsoft, Google, Apache, Cloudflare, and the Python Software Foundation.
  • 05After responsible disclosure, Microsoft and Google confirmed impact, while Cloudflare, Python, and Apache applied hardening or patches.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a should-read finding with broad relevance because it describes a concrete CI/CD weakness affecting more than 300 high-impact GitHub repositories, including projects tied to Microsoft, Google, Apache, Cloudflare, and the Python Software Foundation. The potential impact includes attacker-controlled code execution, credential theft, and open-source supply-chain compromise, though the article does not report active exploitation in the wild.

Reports
  • Cordyceps
    Novee Security
    The article is based on Novee Security's research describing the Cordyceps CI/CD workflow weakness and its impact on major GitHub repositories.
02research
StrikeShark: investigating a new campaign delivering Cobalt Strike through SharkLoader
securelist.com · 2026-06-24 10:00 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGScobalt-strikecolombiafscanhong-kongindonesialebanonnepalnorth-macedoniapillagersearchallserbiasharkloaderstrikesharksyriataiwan
88
In plain words

Researchers found a new worldwide hacking campaign called StrikeShark that uses newly discovered SharkLoader software to secretly install Cobalt Strike, a tool that can give attackers control inside a victim’s computer network. The affected organizations include a diplomatic organization in Indonesia, government organizations in Taiwan, software development companies, and other groups in places such as Hong Kong, Lebanon, Syria, Colombia, North Macedonia, Nepal, and Serbia. This matters because the attackers appear to break in through exposed online business systems or fake installers, then look for passwords and information that could help them move deeper into organizations. The campaign may be aimed at spying or gathering valuable information, and the researchers believe there may be more victims than they have seen so far.

Summary
Kaspersky uncovered StrikeShark, a previously undocumented campaign using the new SharkLoader malware to deploy Cobalt Strike against government, diplomatic, software development, and other organizations across multiple countries. The attackers gain access through vulnerable internet-facing applications and malicious droppers, then use sophisticated in-memory loading, API hooking, and persistence techniques to evade detection.

Kaspersky uncovered a previously undocumented malware family named SharkLoader while investigating activity affecting a diplomatic organization in Indonesia. The malware is used in a broader campaign tracked as StrikeShark to deploy Cobalt Strike Beacon on compromised systems. Related activity affected government organizations in Taiwan, software development companies across multiple countries, and entities in Hong Kong, Lebanon, Syria, Colombia, North Macedonia, Nepal, Serbia, and other locations.

The threat actor used multiple initial access methods, including exploitation of internet-facing applications such as Microsoft Exchange, Microsoft SharePoint, Openfire Server, and GeoServer, as well as custom droppers disguised as legitimate software. Observed vulnerabilities included ProxyLogon, CVE-2023-32315, CVE-2024-36401, and other publicly exploitable flaws in enterprise applications and network appliances. The researchers assessed with medium confidence that the actor primarily relies on publicly available proof-of-concept exploits, and they described the activity as largely opportunistic across industries, regions, and technology environments.

SharkLoader uses DLL sideloading, encrypted components, reflective in-memory loading, API hooks, and memory protection changes to support Cobalt Strike execution and evasion. The campaign also involved persistence through scheduled tasks or registry Run keys, reconnaissance commands, Active Directory enumeration, and credential dumping attempts targeting LSASS and NTDS. Kaspersky found no code or infrastructure overlap tying the activity to a known group, but assessed StrikeShark as a Chinese-speaking threat actor with low confidence based on limited indicators including the use of open-source tools developed by Chinese-speaking developers.

  • The actor appears to rely on publicly available proof-of-concept exploits rather than custom exploit development, because every observed vulnerability had public exploit code available and the victim set was broad and opportunistic.
  • SharkLoader’s persistence differed by intrusion path: dropper-based infections created scheduled tasks automatically, while some server-compromise cases showed the attacker manually creating scheduled tasks or registry Run keys.
  • The malware uses multiple legitimate Windows or application components for DLL sideloading, including SystemSettings.exe with SystemSettings.dll and variants using msedge.dll, PrintDialog.dll, or miracastview.dll, making detection based on a single filename insufficient.
  • The Cobalt Strike payload is protected during sleep periods by changing tracked memory regions from executable permissions to read-write permissions and restoring them afterward, a behavior intended to reduce exposure to memory scanning.
  • Post-compromise activity combined Cobalt Strike and webshell access, showing that responders should not assume removing only the beacon or only the webshell would eliminate the attacker’s access.
Key Points
  • 01Kaspersky uncovered a previously undocumented malware family named SharkLoader while investigating activity affecting a diplomatic organization in Indonesia.
  • 02SharkLoader is used to deploy Cobalt Strike Beacon and has been delivered through exploited internet-facing applications including Microsoft Exchange, Microsoft SharePoint, Openfire Server, and GeoServer, as well as through custom droppers disguised as legitimate software.
  • 03The StrikeShark campaign affected government-related entities, software development companies, and other organizations in countries including Indonesia, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Lebanon, Syria, Colombia, North Macedonia, Nepal, and Serbia.
  • 04SharkLoader uses DLL sideloading, encrypted in-memory components, API hooks, and memory protection changes around Cobalt Strike Beacon sleep periods to support execution and evasion.
  • 05The researchers found no code or infrastructure overlap linking SharkLoader to a known threat actor, but assessed the campaign as likely Chinese-speaking with low confidence based on the open-source tools used after compromise.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific technical report on an active, previously undocumented campaign using new malware, SharkLoader, to deploy Cobalt Strike against government, diplomatic, software development, and other organizations across multiple countries. It is broadly relevant because it documents real-world exploitation of internet-facing applications, persistence methods, evasion techniques, post-compromise activity, and indicators defenders can use to hunt for related intrusions.

Reports
  • Perfect DLL Hijacking
    Elliot Killick
    The article says SharkLoader implements a technique commonly referred to as “Perfect DLL Hijacking,” originally described by security researcher Elliot Killick on his blog.
  • Dynamic-Link Library Best Practices
    Microsoft
    The article cites Microsoft’s Dynamic-Link Library Best Practices to explain the Windows loader lock behavior during DllMain execution.
03research
Trust No One: Automating macOS Privilege Escalation at Scale
xmcyber.com · 2026-06-24 19:01 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSapple-macosblack-hat-usacdhash-cache-exploitationcrowdstrike-falcon-sensorcve-2026-39118endpoint-security-frameworkjavascript-for-automationkandji-mdm-agentnib-payload-injectionobjective-c-runtime-bridgeunited-statesxm-cyberxpc-hunter
84
In plain words

The article describes a way for a normal Mac user, without administrator rights, to make trusted parts of security and device-management apps act on their behalf and shut those tools down. It was tested against products including CrowdStrike Falcon Sensor and Kandji MDM Agent, and the author says similar risks may exist in many Mac apps that let background services talk to user-facing components. This matters because an insider or someone who already has basic access to a Mac could weaken monitoring and management tools before doing more harm, while leaving few obvious signs.

Summary
The article describes a macOS privilege escalation technique that lets a standard user impersonate trusted app components through CDHash cache and NIB injection abuse, enabling privileged XPC calls that can disable EDR and MDM agents. It argues developers must validate XPC client identity on each connection rather than relying on cached code-signing trust.

A novel macOS privilege escalation technique allows standard user accounts to silently disable enterprise security products, including major EDR and MDM solutions, without administrator credentials, kernel exploits, or security alerts. The article says the technique abuses how macOS XPC services establish trust boundaries by chaining CDHash kernel cache exploitation with NIB payload injection to impersonate trusted application components. As a result, an unprivileged user can invoke privileged XPC methods with no authentication.

The research was validated against CrowdStrike Falcon Sensor and Kandji MDM Agent. In the CrowdStrike case, full sensor unloading was achieved from a standard user account, terminating detection, process monitoring, and network visibility, and the article says detections and preventions were added for supported Falcon sensor versions. In the Kandji case, a two-phase XPC chain permanently deactivated the agent and terminated the Endpoint Security Framework extension, and the issue was fixed with CVE-2026-39118 assigned.

Technically, the attack relies on launching a valid signed application to populate the kernel CDHash cache, then manipulating the application bundle to inject malicious NIB payloads while retaining trusted process context. The payload can then enumerate and call privileged management methods such as runProcessWithCommand:, terminateAppsAndAgents:, and ceaseSystemExtensionWithReply:. The article recommends that developers validate client identity directly in XPC listener logic using xpc_connection_set_peer_code_signing_requirement() or by re-validating signatures with SecCodeCheckValidity() and audit-token-based checks.

  • The attack does not require breaking a product’s code or using a kernel exploit; it abuses trusted operating system behavior, which the article says helps it avoid standard exploit signatures and obvious event log artifacts.
  • The most important trust failure is not simply that privileged XPC methods exist, but that products may trust a client because it appears to be one of their own signed components instead of validating the actual caller during the live connection.
  • The exposed methods found during testing were built-in management functions such as terminating apps, running commands, and stopping system extensions, meaning the targeted tools could be made to disable themselves through their own intended maintenance workflows.
  • The article presents a concrete remediation path for developers: enforce code-signing requirements on the active XPC connection or re-check the caller’s on-disk signature using the incoming connection’s audit token before exposing any protocol methods.
Key Points
  • 01The article describes a macOS privilege escalation technique that can allow standard user accounts to silently disable enterprise security products, including EDR and MDM solutions, without administrator credentials or kernel exploits.
  • 02The technique chains CDHash kernel cache exploitation with NIB payload injection to impersonate trusted application components and invoke privileged XPC methods without authentication.
  • 03The research was validated against CrowdStrike Falcon Sensor, where full sensor unloading was achieved from a standard user account, and Kandji MDM Agent, where permanent agent deactivation was achieved through a two-phase XPC chain.
  • 04The author built XPC Hunter, an open-source automated framework intended to discover exploitable XPC privilege escalation surfaces across installed macOS applications at scale.
  • 05The article recommends that developers validate client identity inside XPC listener logic using xpc_connection_set_peer_code_signing_requirement() or by re-validating signatures with SecCodeCheckValidity() and audit-token-based checks.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific technical finding with broad relevance to macOS endpoint security, including validated impact against named EDR and MDM products and a concrete CVE for Kandji. It is not described as active exploitation in the wild or an urgent patch advisory, but the ability for standard users to disable security agents makes it important for security professionals to understand.

Reports
  • CVE-2026-39118
    CVE.org
    The article links to the CVE record assigned to the Kandji MDM Agent issue described as fixed.
04news
DoJ Seizes Huione Cloud Account Tied to Cyber Scam Money Laundering
thehackernews.com · 2026-06-24 08:55 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGScambodiachinadeepfake-impersonationfincenh-pay-service-plchaowang-guaranteehuione-grouphuione-guaranteepig-butcheringprince-groupsoutheast-asiatelegramunited-statesus-department-of-justiceus-treasury
82
In plain words

The U.S. Justice Department seized a cloud computing account used by companies connected to Cambodia-based HuiOne Group, saying it helped move and hide money stolen through online scams and fake cryptocurrency investment schemes. The services were tied to a Telegram marketplace that sold stolen personal and financial data, scam websites, money-moving help, and tools for fake video or voice impersonation. This matters because Southeast Asian scam centers and related groups have targeted Americans and others at large scale, while also being linked to trafficking and abuse of workers forced into scam operations. U.S. officials also announced new actions against people and companies connected to Prince Group, showing a wider effort to cut off the money channels behind these scams.

Summary
The U.S. Justice Department seized a cloud account used by HuiOne Group subsidiaries to support money laundering infrastructure tied to crypto fraud, scam compounds, and an illicit Telegram marketplace. Authorities also expanded pressure on related Southeast Asian scam networks, including sanctions against Prince Group-linked individuals and entities.

The U.S. Department of Justice announced the seizure of a cloud computing account used by subsidiaries of Cambodia-based HuiOne Group. The account allegedly hosted backend infrastructure for services that helped transfer proceeds from cryptocurrency investment frauds, cyber scams, and other criminal activity on cryptocurrency blockchains into the legitimate banking sector undetected. The action came as the U.S. Treasury announced sanctions against nine individuals and 26 entities linked to Prince Group.

The seized infrastructure supported HuiOne Guarantee, also known as Haowang Guarantee, an illicit Telegram-based marketplace that handled billions of dollars in transactions between 2021 and 2025. The marketplace offered personal and financial data, money laundering services, web development for fraudulent investment platforms and phishing websites, human trafficking procurement, and tools for face swapping, voice cloning, and deepfake impersonation during video calls. The Justice Department said HuiOne Guarantee also provided escrow services for criminals, including money launderers moving cryptocurrency.

The article says HuiOne announced it was ceasing operations in May 2025, but Flare found that more than 30 marketplaces have since emerged to fill the gap, including operators building proprietary messaging platforms to bypass Telegram bans. FinCEN assessed H-Pay Service PLC as a primary money laundering concern to address HuiOne Group’s attempts to circumvent being cut off from the U.S. financial system. Elliptic said HuiOne had received more than $31 billion in cryptoasset transactions by the time it was forced offline, making it the largest illicit online marketplace ever recorded.

  • The HuiOne ecosystem was not limited to moving stolen money; it also supported the operational needs of scam centers by offering stolen data, fake investment site development, impersonation tools, and even goods described as being used to control trafficked workers.
  • Enforcement actions have disrupted visible infrastructure, but Flare reported that more than 30 successor marketplaces emerged after HuiOne announced it was ceasing operations, including efforts to move away from Telegram to proprietary messaging platforms.
  • Elliptic described HuiOne as having received more than $31 billion in cryptoasset transactions, making it larger than Silk Road and AlphaBay combined by a factor of more than 25.
  • U.S. authorities are targeting both the technical infrastructure and financial access points of the ecosystem, shown by the DoJ cloud account seizure and FinCEN’s action against H-Pay Service PLC as a HuiOne successor concern.
Key Points
  • 01The U.S. Department of Justice announced the seizure of a cloud computing account used by subsidiaries of Cambodia-based HuiOne Group to host backend infrastructure for alleged money laundering services.
  • 02The seized account supported HuiOne Guarantee, also known as Haowang Guarantee, an illicit Telegram-based marketplace that handled billions of dollars in transactions between 2021 and 2025.
  • 03The marketplace offered personal and financial data, money laundering services, fraudulent investment and phishing website services, human trafficking procurement, and software for face swapping, voice cloning, and deepfake impersonation during video calls.
  • 04FinCEN assessed H-Pay Service PLC as a primary money laundering concern to guard against HuiOne Group’s attempts to circumvent being cut off from the U.S. financial system.
  • 05The U.S. Treasury imposed sanctions against nine individuals and 26 entities linked to Prince Group, which had been classified as a Transnational Criminal Organization for its role in scam compounds, fraud, and money laundering.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This reports a specific U.S. law enforcement and sanctions action against infrastructure allegedly supporting large-scale cyber scam money laundering, with named entities and substantial financial impact. It is broadly relevant to security professionals tracking cybercrime ecosystems, crypto-enabled fraud, and Southeast Asian scam operations, though it is not an urgent vulnerability or active exploitation alert.

Reports
  • July 2024 analysis of HuiOne merchants
    Elliptic
    The article cites a July 2024 Elliptic analysis that found HuiOne merchants marketed items such as tear gas, electric batons, and electronic shackles for scam compound operators.
  • Analysis of Chinese-language guarantee marketplaces after HuiOne
    Flare
    The article references Flare analysis reporting that more than 30 successor marketplaces emerged after HuiOne announced it was ceasing operations.
05news
German rail services resume after wireless communications outage
therecord.media · 2026-06-24 14:15 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSdeutsche-bahnenglandgermanygsm-rs-bahnunited-kingdom
78
In plain words

Germany’s national rail operator, Deutsche Bahn, had to stop trains across the country for about two hours after a failure in the wireless system used by train staff and control centers to communicate safely. Long-distance, regional, and S-Bahn commuter passengers were affected, with delays and cancellations continuing even after service restarted. The incident matters because it shows how a technical problem in one critical communications system can quickly disrupt travel for millions of people and affect an entire national transport network.

Summary
Deutsche Bahn restored rail services after a nationwide outage in its GSM-R railway radio system halted trains across Germany for about two hours. The company said the disruption was caused by a technical failure during scheduled component replacement, not a reported cyberattack.

Germany’s state-owned rail operator Deutsche Bahn restored train services early Wednesday after a technical failure in its railway communications network brought rail traffic across the country to a standstill for roughly two hours overnight. The outage began late Tuesday and disrupted both long-distance and regional services, including S-Bahn commuter services connecting major cities with surrounding suburbs. Deutsche Bahn said service was gradually resuming but warned passengers to expect lingering delays and cancellations.

Deutsche Bahn said the incident stemmed from a malfunction in its GSM-R digital railway radio system, which is used by train drivers, dispatchers, and signaling systems to coordinate rail operations safely. Philipp Nagl, chief executive of Deutsche Bahn’s infrastructure subsidiary, said the disruption occurred during a scheduled replacement of a technical component and that the company was analyzing the exact cause. The company has not indicated that the incident was the result of a cyberattack and has described it as a technical failure.

GSM-R is a specialized wireless communications standard widely used by European rail operators and is built on aging 2G technology. The article notes that GSM-R is gradually being phased out in favor of a next-generation railway communications platform based on 5G technology, though the transition is expected to take years. Deutsche Bahn apologized to affected travelers and said it would provide hotel and taxi vouchers where necessary and arrange alternative transportation when possible.

  • A scheduled replacement of a technical component was enough to disrupt Deutsche Bahn’s GSM-R radio system nationwide, showing that routine maintenance on critical rail communications can have broad operational consequences.
  • The outage highlights the operational risk of continued reliance on GSM-R, which the article describes as an aging 2G-based system that is being phased out only gradually over several years.
  • Similar GSM-R-related disruptions in southern England and across Britain indicate that failures in this railway communications standard have affected multiple national rail networks, not just Deutsche Bahn.
  • Even after the technical issue was fixed and services resumed, Deutsche Bahn still expected lingering delays and cancellations, showing that short outages in rail communications can have effects beyond the immediate downtime.
Key Points
  • 01Deutsche Bahn restored train services early Wednesday after a technical failure in its railway communications network halted rail traffic across Germany for roughly two hours overnight.
  • 02The outage affected long-distance and regional trains as well as S-Bahn commuter services, and Deutsche Bahn warned passengers to expect lingering delays and cancellations after service resumed.
  • 03Deutsche Bahn said the incident stemmed from a malfunction in its GSM-R digital railway radio system, which is used by train drivers, dispatchers, and signaling systems to coordinate rail operations safely.
  • 04Philipp Nagl said the disruption occurred during a scheduled replacement of a technical component, and Deutsche Bahn said it was still analyzing the exact cause.
  • 05The company has not indicated that the incident was the result of a cyberattack and has described it as a technical failure.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This reports a specific, nationwide disruption to critical rail communications infrastructure that halted German train services, making it broadly relevant to security professionals focused on operational resilience and critical infrastructure. It is not described as a cyberattack or active exploitation, so it is important but not must-read urgent.

Indian auto giant Bajaj Auto hit by ransomware incident

TAGSbajaj-autobajaj-auto-technology-limitedcanadaeuropeindiatata-electronicsunited-statesworld-leaks
78
In plain words

Bajaj Auto, one of India’s largest vehicle makers, said it was hit by a ransomware incident that affected its own systems and those of Bajaj Auto Technology Limited, its technology, engineering, and research subsidiary. The company said it noticed the problem Tuesday morning, took steps to limit the damage, and that its response had so far been successful. It has not said who was behind it, whether any information was stolen, or whether money was demanded. This matters because Bajaj Auto makes motorcycles, scooters, and commercial vehicles, and the incident comes as other major Indian manufacturers have also reported cyber problems.

Summary
Bajaj Auto disclosed a ransomware incident affecting its operations and technology subsidiary, saying containment and mitigation efforts are underway and so far successful. The company has not identified the attackers or said whether data was stolen or a ransom was demanded.

Bajaj Auto disclosed on Tuesday that it had been hit by a ransomware attack affecting its operations and a technology-focused subsidiary. The company said in a regulatory filing that it became aware of the incident on Tuesday morning and took precautionary measures to contain its impact. It said its technical team and cybersecurity experts responded immediately, and that mitigation efforts had so far been “successful.”

The incident affected systems at Bajaj Auto and Bajaj Auto Technology Limited, a subsidiary focused on technology development, engineering and research. Bajaj Auto did not disclose the identity of the threat actor, whether any data was stolen, or whether the attackers issued a ransom demand. The company did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Bajaj Auto is one of India’s largest vehicle manufacturers, producing motorcycles, scooters and commercial vehicles, and is also described as the world’s largest manufacturer of three-wheelers. The disclosure comes amid a series of cyber incidents involving major Indian manufacturers, including a cybersecurity incident confirmed earlier in the week by Tata Electronics. The article states there is no indication so far that the ransomware attack on Bajaj Auto is connected to the Tata Electronics incident.

  • The incident reached Bajaj Auto Technology Limited, meaning the impact was not limited to business operations but also involved a subsidiary focused on technology development, engineering, and research.
  • Bajaj Auto disclosed the incident through a regulatory filing on the same day it became aware of it, showing the event was considered significant enough for investor-facing notification.
  • Although the disclosure came amid other cyber incidents involving major Indian manufacturers, the article states there is no indication that Bajaj Auto’s ransomware incident is connected to the Tata Electronics incident.
Key Points
  • 01Bajaj Auto disclosed in a regulatory filing that it was hit by a ransomware attack affecting its operations and Bajaj Auto Technology Limited.
  • 02The company said it became aware of the incident on Tuesday morning and took precautionary measures to contain its impact.
  • 03Bajaj Auto said its technical team and cybersecurity experts responded immediately and that mitigation efforts had so far been successful.
  • 04Bajaj Auto did not disclose the threat actor’s identity, whether any data was stolen, or whether a ransom demand was issued.
  • 05The disclosure follows other cyber incidents involving major Indian manufacturers, including a cybersecurity incident confirmed earlier in the week by Tata Electronics.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific ransomware incident affecting Bajaj Auto, one of India’s largest vehicle manufacturers, and its technology-focused subsidiary. It is broadly relevant because it involves a major manufacturer and appears in the context of other cyber incidents affecting Indian manufacturers, though the article provides limited technical detail and no confirmed data theft or named threat actor.

01news
A Free Gmail Account Impersonating an Internal Employee Asked Payroll to Change a Bank Account
ironscales.com · 2026-06-24 11:00 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSbusiness-email-compromisecisacredential-harvestingdirect-deposit-frauddisplay-name-spoofingdkimdmarcgmailironscales-adaptive-aimitre-attack-t1534mitre-attack-t1598payroll-redirectspf
72
In plain words

An attacker used a free Gmail account to pretend to be a real employee at a financial institution and asked the payroll contact to change that employee’s direct-deposit bank account. The email looked normal, had no links or attachments, and passed standard email checks because the only trick was showing the real employee’s name while sending from an unrelated Gmail address. This matters because payroll and finance teams could send someone’s pay to an attacker-controlled account unless banking changes are verified another way, such as calling the employee at a known number.

Summary
A payroll redirect BEC used a free Gmail account with a spoofed display name to impersonate an internal employee and request a direct-deposit bank change, bypassing SPF, DKIM, and DMARC because there were no links, attachments, or malicious code. The key defense is mandatory out-of-band verification for any banking-change request.

An attacker used a free Gmail account to impersonate a named internal employee at a financial institution and asked the payroll contact to update direct-deposit banking information. The message addressed the payroll contact by first name, used a polite and professional tone, and was signed with the impersonated employee’s full name. The sending address was churchoffice11011[at]gmail[.]com, which had no relationship to the impersonated employee or the organization.

The email contained no links, attachments, or malicious code, and SPF, DKIM, and DMARC all passed for Gmail infrastructure. The article explains that the deception was entirely in the display name, because email authentication checks do not determine whether a visible sender name matches a real employee. IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the message at 75% confidence as BEC and Vendor Email Compromise based on the mismatch between the claimed identity and the unrelated consumer Gmail address.

The significance of the attack is that a payroll redirect request can cause financial loss without malware, credential theft, or a technical payload. If processed, the fraudulent banking update could redirect funds to an attacker-controlled account during the next payroll cycle. The article recommends requiring out-of-band verification for all direct-deposit, ACH, or wire changes, training payroll and finance teams to inspect the actual From address, and treating first-contact addresses as high risk for sensitive requests.

  • Email authentication can fully pass while still leaving display-name impersonation untouched, because DMARC checks alignment of authenticated domains rather than whether the visible sender name matches a real person.
  • A no-payload payroll redirect email can create financial loss through a normal business workflow, making sandboxing, attachment scanning, and URL analysis irrelevant for this type of fraud.
  • A consumer email address making a first-time sensitive HR or finance request is treated in the article as a high-risk signal even when the message has no known malicious indicators.
  • A mandatory phone verification using a known, pre-existing number can neutralize the display-name impersonation technique regardless of how convincing the email appears.
Key Points
  • 01An attacker used a free Gmail account, churchoffice11011[at]gmail[.]com, with a display name matching a real internal employee at a financial institution to contact the payroll recipient.
  • 02The email asked for a direct-deposit bank account update and contained no links, attachments, malicious code, urgency marker, deadline, or threat.
  • 03SPF, DKIM, and DMARC all passed for Gmail infrastructure because the message was legitimately sent through Gmail and the deception was limited to the display name.
  • 04IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the message at 75% confidence and labeled it as BEC and Vendor Email Compromise based on the mismatch between the claimed employee identity and the unrelated consumer Gmail address.
  • 05The article recommends out-of-band verification for all direct-deposit, ACH, or wire changes by calling the employee at a known, pre-existing number before processing the request.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

The article describes a specific payroll redirect BEC attempt that bypassed standard email authentication by using display-name impersonation and a free Gmail account. It is useful for security professionals because the case highlights a common no-payload fraud pattern and reinforces actionable controls, but it is not a breaking, large-scale, or named threat actor event.

  • MITRE ATT&CK T1534

  • CISA guidance on BEC
    CISA
    The article cites CISA guidance on BEC as emphasizing out-of-band verification as the primary control.
  • Microsoft Digital Defense Report 2024

02news
UK Cybercrime Journal: Hargreaves Landsdown Extortion Attempt by Bashe
blog.bushidotoken.net · 2026-06-24 08:00 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSapt73bashecloudsekdarkforumsdata-theft-extortioneraleignhargreaves-lansdownhave-i-been-pwnedpeople-data-labsunited-kingdomverifications-io
72
In plain words

The article says Hargreaves Lansdown, a major UK investment platform, had past website and app problems, then a criminal group called Bashe claimed it had stolen data on more than 658,000 customers. The article’s analysis says this claim was probably fake, because the sample data appeared to come from older public leaks rather than from Hargreaves Lansdown. Customers and the company were still affected because the false claim could cause fear, damage trust, and pressure the company to pay money. The wider concern is that criminals can use ordinary technical problems and public anxiety to make fake hacks seem believable.

Summary
Hargreaves Lansdown was named by Bashe/APT73 in an alleged data-theft extortion attempt, but analysis suggests the claimed customer database was fabricated using previously leaked public breach data. The key takeaway is that threat actors are increasingly exploiting real IT outages and customer anxiety to create convincing fake breach narratives for extortion.

Hargreaves Lansdown, the UK-based investment platform, experienced a series of customer-facing issues and claims between September 2025 and May 2026. In September 2025, some customers saw incorrect pension and ISA balances that made it appear large sums had been withdrawn, but the company said this was a temporary technical issue lasting 45 minutes and restored all balances. In March 2026, customers had website and app access problems during market volatility, and the company said there was no evidence of a cyber incident or data breach.

On 27 April 2026, Bashe listed Hargreaves Lansdown on its Tor data leak site and claimed to have stolen a customer database of 658,259 unique users. On 8 May 2026, alleged Hargreaves Lansdown data appeared on DarkForums, with a sample containing addresses, names, emails, phone numbers, and dates of birth. The sample did not include Hargreaves Lansdown customer account numbers or transaction details.

The article says analysis of the sample data found the posted email addresses appeared in the Verifications.io breach and the People Data Labs customer breach, which it describes as unusual for recently leaked data. It assesses with high confidence that the alleged Hargreaves Lansdown breach was fabricated, based on the sample analysis and Bashe’s reported history of false breach claims. The article concludes that Bashe likely used Hargreaves Lansdown’s recent outages and customer concerns to create a plausible but false extortion narrative.

  • The article treats the overlap between the alleged sample emails and older Verifications.io and People Data Labs breaches as a practical way to test whether a claimed fresh breach is fabricated.
  • The article notes that genuinely stolen data from a financial trading platform would likely be valuable on criminal markets, making a free dump on a forum or Tor site an indicator that the claim may be false.
  • The article highlights that public communication should explicitly separate ordinary IT outages from cyber incidents, because criminals can use unresolved customer anxiety to make fake breach claims more believable.
  • The article warns that even defensive actions such as mass password resets can unintentionally strengthen a fake breach narrative if they are not paired with clear, tailored communication.
Key Points
  • 01Hargreaves Lansdown experienced customer-facing technical issues in September 2025 and March 2026, including incorrect displayed balances and access problems with its website and app.
  • 02On 27 April 2026, Bashe listed Hargreaves Lansdown on its Tor data leak site and claimed to have stolen a customer database containing 658,259 unique users.
  • 03On 8 May 2026, alleged Hargreaves Lansdown data appeared on DarkForums, with a posted sample containing names, addresses, emails, phone numbers, and dates of birth but no customer account numbers or transaction details.
  • 04The article states that sample email addresses appeared in the Verifications.io breach and the People Data Labs customer breach, which it says likely indicates the Bashe and DarkForums data was fake.
  • 05The article assesses with high confidence that the alleged Hargreaves Lansdown data breach was fabricated and that Bashe used the company’s recent IT outages to support a false extortion narrative.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific, concrete case involving a named threat actor’s alleged extortion attempt against a major UK financial platform, with useful indicators for assessing fake breach claims. It is not immediately urgent because the article assesses the breach as fabricated, but it remains valuable for security professionals handling incident response, threat intelligence, and crisis communications.

Reports
  • Unmasking Media-Hungry Ransomware Groups: Bashe/APT73
    CloudSEK
    The article cites CloudSEK analysts as finding that APT73 fabricates high-profile breach claims to attract affiliates and build credibility.
03research
Disposable Tooling: Building LLM-Generated Mythic Agents from Prompt to Deployment
specterops.io · 2026-06-24 17:29 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSapfellclaude-codecodexdockergpt-5-4-cybergpt-5-5-cyber-previewlabkitmythicmythic-climythicdopencodeopus-4-6oraclerabbitmqwindows-11
68
In plain words

The article describes SpecterOps experimenting with AI systems that can create short-lived Mythic agents, which are programs used in security testing, from a simple request through testing and deployment in only a few hours. The author built extra testing tools so the AI could check whether these programs actually worked instead of just producing broken code. This matters because both defenders and real attackers can use similar AI-assisted methods to quickly make new throwaway tools, making older ways of spotting known programs by fixed signatures much less reliable.

Summary
SpecterOps demonstrates that with the right testing harness and feedback loops, LLMs can generate and validate disposable Mythic C2 agents from prompt to deployment in a few hours. The key takeaway is that rapidly generated, single-use offensive tooling is now practical, making static implant signatures increasingly unreliable for defenders.

The article describes the author’s experiments with generating “disposable tooling” by using large language models to build Mythic agents from an initial prompt through deployment. The goal was to create a new agent with no human in the loop between the initial specification and a tested, shippable result. Mythic was chosen because its architecture separates agent development from the underlying command-and-control framework.

The first attempts produced code that looked clean and compiled, but the resulting agent did not work because of missing packages, incorrect Docker paths, hallucinated Mythic RPC methods, and misunderstanding of the key-exchange process. The author improved results by adding Mythic documentation as a development skill, then created an “Oracle” harness with tiered testing against a mock Mythic server, a live Mythic server, and a Windows test host. Additional tools, including LabKit, Mythicd, mythic-cli, and a QA sub-agent, were added to improve debugging, deployment, process monitoring, and independent validation.

With the improved harness, the author reports generating multiple basic Stage-0 Mythic agents in different languages, usually in about 1.5 to just over 2 hours. The article also describes porting the workflow from Claude Code and Opus 4.6 to GPT-5.4-Cyber and GPT-5.5-Cyber-Preview, with generated agents tested end-to-end. The author concludes that rapidly generated single-use agents are now practical and that static signatures or YARA rules focused on implant detection are becoming increasingly outdated.

  • End-to-end testing, rather than better prompting alone, was the turning point that made LLM-generated Mythic agents reliable enough to be delivered without manual steering.
  • Providing the LLM with purpose-built interfaces such as mythic-cli and Mythicd reduced failure loops caused by the model misusing Mythic’s GraphQL API, stale container logs, or temporary Docker container changes.
  • A separate QA sub-agent with a clean context window helped counter the main model’s tendency to assume functionality worked based on prior context instead of validating every command.
  • The author found that disposable agents could be generated across multiple programming languages, suggesting the chosen language was less of a barrier than the quality of the harness and validation workflow.
  • The article argues that static signatures and YARA rules focused on implant detection are becoming increasingly outdated because new single-use agents can now be generated quickly.
Key Points
  • 01The author explored generating “disposable tooling” by using LLMs to build Mythic agents from an initial prompt through deployment.
  • 02The first attempt with Claude Code and Opus 4.6 produced code that compiled and deployed as a Docker image, but the generated Mythic agent failed because of missing packages, incorrect paths, hallucinated Mythic RPC methods, and misunderstandings of Mythic key exchange.
  • 03Adding Mythic documentation as an Anthropic Skill improved results and helped produce a working proof-of-concept agent, but still required some manual intervention and steering.
  • 04The author created an Oracle harness with tiered testing, including a mock Mythic server, a live Mythic server and Windows test host, LabKit, Mythicd, and a QA sub-agent to improve validation and feedback.
  • 05Using the improved harness, the author generated multiple basic Stage-0 Mythic agents in different languages with Claude and GPT Cyber models, typically in about 1.5 to just over 2 hours.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a concrete technical analysis of using LLMs and testing harnesses to generate Mythic agents, with practical relevance for red teams and defenders thinking about disposable tooling. It is not a breaking incident, active exploitation report, breach, or advisory, so it is useful but not urgent reading.

04news
DraftKings hacker 'Snoopy' sentenced to 18 months in prison
bleepingcomputer.com · 2026-06-24 21:55 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGScredential-stuffingdraftkingsgoat-shopjoseph-garrisonkamerin-stokesnathan-austadsnoopythe-mfn-plugunited-statesus-department-of-justice
67
In plain words

A 21-year-old Minnesota man who used the name “Snoopy” was sentenced to 18 months in prison for helping break into DraftKings customer accounts in 2022. The group got into about 60,000 accounts, added payment methods they controlled to 1,600 of them, and stole $600,000 from users of the fantasy sports and betting platform. The case matters because it shows how reused or weak login details can let criminals take over real people’s accounts, steal money, and sell access to those accounts online.

Summary
Nathan Austad, a 21-year-old hacker known as “Snoopy,” was sentenced to 18 months in prison for helping compromise tens of thousands of DraftKings accounts in a 2022 credential-stuffing scheme. The attackers stole about $600,000, and Austad was also ordered to pay forfeiture and restitution.

Nathan Austad, a 21-year-old from Minnesota who used the alias “Snoopy,” was sentenced to 18 months in prison for his role in the November 2022 cyberattack on DraftKings accounts. In December 2025, he pleaded guilty to conspiracy to commit computer intrusion and admitted that he and co-conspirators compromised 60,000 DraftKings user accounts. During the attack, the hackers added payment methods under their control to 1,600 accounts and stole $600,000.

DraftKings disclosed in November 2022 that hackers accessed customer accounts through credential stuffing attacks that exploited weak passwords or reused login credentials. The company initially reported that less than $300,000 had been stolen from affected customers, and later disclosed that 67,995 customer accounts had been compromised. U.S. authorities said Austad operated his own shop, named after Snoopy from the Peanuts comic strip, where he sold access to stolen accounts and also used other platforms for the same purpose.

The U.S. Department of Justice said Austad’s cryptocurrency accounts received approximately \(465,000 in assets, though it did not disclose how much the hackers earned from selling account access. Joseph Garrison received an 18-month prison sentence in January 2024 for his role in the scheme, while Kamerin Stokes received a 30-month sentence in April 2026. In addition to prison, Austad received three years of supervised release and was ordered to pay \)463,684 in forfeiture and $1,327,061 in restitution.

  • The reported scale of the incident grew over time, from DraftKings initially saying less than $300,000 was stolen to prosecutors later describing $600,000 stolen and about 60,000 accounts compromised.
  • The attackers did not need to break into DraftKings by exploiting a newly described software flaw; the article says they accessed accounts through reused or weak customer login credentials.
  • The financial penalties imposed on Austad were much larger than the $600,000 described as stolen in the attack, including $463,684 in forfeiture and $1,327,061 in restitution.
  • The case shows that selling access to already-compromised accounts was treated as a central part of the scheme, with Austad operating his own shop and also using other platforms to sell stolen account access.
Key Points
  • 01Nathan Austad, a 21-year-old from Minnesota who used the alias “Snoopy,” was sentenced to 18 months in prison for his role in hacking DraftKings accounts in the November 2022 cyberattack.
  • 02Austad pleaded guilty in December 2025 to conspiracy to commit computer intrusion and admitted that he and co-conspirators compromised 60,000 DraftKings user accounts.
  • 03During the attack, the hackers added payment methods under their control to 1,600 accounts and stole $600,000.
  • 04U.S. authorities said Austad operated his own shop named after Snoopy where he sold access to stolen accounts, and his cryptocurrency accounts received approximately $465,000 in assets.
  • 05In addition to prison, Austad received three years of supervised release and was ordered to pay $463,684 in forfeiture and $1,327,061 in restitution.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific, concrete cybercrime case involving credential stuffing against DraftKings accounts and sentencing of a participant, but it is primarily legal follow-up to a 2022 incident rather than an active threat or urgent defensive issue. It is useful for understanding account-takeover impact and enforcement outcomes, but has limited immediate operational urgency for most security professionals.

05announcement
Unlocking the Cloudflare app ecosystem with OAuth for all
blog.cloudflare.com · 2026-06-24 06:00 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGScloudflarecloudflare-queuescloudflare-workersmcpoauthory-hydraplanetscalewrangler
64
In plain words

Cloudflare is now letting all customers create their own sign-in permission flows so other apps can ask for limited access to Cloudflare accounts instead of using harder-to-manage access keys. This affects developers, Cloudflare customers, and companies building tools that connect to Cloudflare. It matters because users can more clearly see what an app wants to do, take access away more easily, and have better control over what outside applications can do with their Cloudflare data.

Summary
Cloudflare has opened self-managed OAuth to all customers, enabling developers to build delegated-access integrations with clearer consent and revocation instead of relying on API tokens. The launch followed major OAuth engine upgrades and migrations to improve security, reliability, and performance.

Cloudflare announced self-managed OAuth, allowing customers to create and manage their own OAuth clients for delegated access to the Cloudflare API. Third-party OAuth had previously been limited to a small number of manually onboarded integrations, which meant many developers had to rely on API tokens. The change is intended to support SaaS integrations, internal developer platforms, and agentic tools while giving users clearer consent, easier revocation, and more control over application access.

To support the release, Cloudflare upgraded its underlying OAuth engine, Hydra, through staged 1.X and 2.X upgrades. The team rewrote SQL migrations to avoid exclusive locks on critical tables and built a custom Hydra version to avoid SELECT * deserialization issues during schema changes. For the larger 2.X upgrade, Cloudflare used a blue-green strategy, increased token expiry to reduce token writes, and used Cloudflare Queues to capture and replay revocation events so revoked applications would not regain access.

The upgrade process included database copying, targeted data cleanup, coordinated cutovers across Hydra and internal systems, and post-cutover monitoring. Cloudflare observed and mitigated issues including stricter refresh token invalidation behavior, increased 403s caused by a migration-related session state problem, and some client-specific fixes. After completion, Cloudflare reported improved OAuth stability and performance, including lower API P95 latency, reduced memory usage, lower heap allocation, fewer goroutines, and lower CPU usage.

  • Cloudflare treated revocation preservation as a critical security requirement during migration, using Cloudflare Queues to replay revocation events so that applications users had revoked would not regain access after cutover.
  • Even an intermediate 1.X Hydra upgrade required custom SQL migrations and a custom Hydra build because default migrations could lock critical tables and the older SDK’s SELECT * behavior could break during schema changes.
  • A stricter refresh token invalidation behavior in Hydra caused increased errors for high-volume clients such as Wrangler and MCP clients, leading Cloudflare to add refresh token coalescing before relying on Hydra 2.X’s configurable refresh token grace period.
  • The 2.X migration exposed a hidden dependency between Hydra consent session state and Cloudflare’s authorization service, where corrupted session validity caused overeager cleanup of OAuth policy data and an increase in 403 errors.
Key Points
  • 01Cloudflare announced self-managed OAuth so customers can create and manage their own OAuth clients for delegated access to the Cloudflare API.
  • 02Before this release, third-party OAuth was limited to a small number of manually onboarded integrations, so many developers building integrations had to rely on API tokens.
  • 03Cloudflare updated its OAuth consent experience, added dashboard revocation, and made app ownership more visible to help users understand and control application access.
  • 04Cloudflare upgraded its underlying Hydra OAuth engine using a staged approach, including custom migrations and a blue-green migration strategy to reduce user impact and preserve revocations.
  • 05After the Hydra upgrade, Cloudflare reported improved performance metrics, including lower API P95 latency, memory use, heap allocation, goroutines, and CPU usage.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific Cloudflare platform release with useful security-relevant details about delegated access, consent, revocation, and OAuth migration safeguards. It is not urgent threat reporting, an active exploitation case, or a critical patch, so it is mainly a may-read for professionals working with Cloudflare integrations or OAuth systems.

06announcement
Why Is an AI Image Company Building a Body Scanner?
health-isac.org · 2026-06-24 19:43 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSfdahealth-isachealthcare-infosecurityhipaamidjourneyphil-englert
64
In plain words

Midjourney, known for making images from text, says it is building a full-body ultrasound scanner for use in consumer spas, even though it does not yet have FDA clearance. Health-ISAC warned that each scan could create a huge amount of personal body data and that the many parts inside the scanner could create more ways for someone to tamper with it. People who use the service may be affected because a spa that takes direct payment may not have the same health privacy duties as a doctor or hospital, so customers need to know what protections they do or do not have.

Summary
Midjourney plans to roll out a consumer full-body ultrasound scanner in spas without FDA clearance yet, raising significant cybersecurity, data volume, and regulatory concerns. Health-ISAC warns that users may not receive HIPAA protections if they are treated as customers rather than patients.

Midjourney, the company best known for generating images from text prompts, has announced its first hardware product: a full-body ultrasound scanner intended for deployment in consumer spas. The article says the company plans a consumer rollout, with no FDA clearance yet. Phil Englert, vice president of medical device security at Health-ISAC, said innovation in healthcare can come from unusual places, but he raised potential data security concerns.

Englert said a single full-body ultrasound study can produce roughly 800 terabytes of data, about three times what a midsize hospital generates in a day. Because the device is built from thousands of elements that each emit and receive sound, he said every element adds to the attack surface. He said a threat model would need to account for whether the transducers could be manipulated during a scan.

The article notes that HIPAA applies only to covered entities such as providers that bill insurance, health plans, clearinghouses, and their business associates. Englert said a spa that charges customers directly without involving insurance falls outside those rules, and people using such services should understand whether they are customers or patients and what protections that distinction provides. He also warned that a common mistake in medical-device development is leaving cybersecurity until it is too late.

  • The spa setting could create a privacy gap because customers who pay directly may not receive the same HIPAA protections that would apply in many traditional healthcare settings.
  • The scanner’s data scale is itself a security concern, since one full-body ultrasound study could generate more data than a midsize hospital produces in an entire day.
  • The device’s physical design changes the threat model because thousands of individual sound-emitting and receiving elements each add potential points of concern.
  • Health-ISAC’s warning highlights that cybersecurity needs to be addressed early in medical-device development, not added after the product is nearly ready for rollout.
Key Points
  • 01Midjourney has announced its first hardware product, a full-body ultrasound scanner that it plans to deploy in consumer spas.
  • 02The article states that Midjourney has no FDA clearance yet for the planned consumer rollout.
  • 03Phil Englert of Health-ISAC said a single full-body ultrasound study can produce roughly 800 terabytes of data, about three times what a midsize hospital generates in a day.
  • 04Englert said the scanner’s thousands of sound-emitting and receiving elements add to the attack surface, and a threat model would need to consider whether transducers could be manipulated during a scan.
  • 05Englert said a spa that charges customers directly without involving insurance may fall outside HIPAA rules, so users should understand whether they are customers or patients and what protections apply.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This covers a specific product announcement with concrete security and privacy concerns around a planned consumer full-body ultrasound scanner. It is relevant for medical device and healthcare security professionals, but it does not report active exploitation, a breach, a vulnerability, or an urgent patch.

01opinion
How much cyber risk does AI create for organizations? 457 million security issues. Here’s what you can do about it.
tenable.com · 2026-06-24 13:00 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGScisa-kevclawdbotcve-2017-0144cve-2021-44228cve-2024-21762fortinet-fortioshexa-ailog4shellmoltbotopenclawtelegramtenabletenable-oneverizon-dbirwannacry
58
In plain words

Tenable says it found 457 million AI-related security problems across more than 7,000 organizations in 30 days, often caused by poorly set up AI tools, hidden use of unapproved AI, and unmanaged connections rather than only known software flaws. This affects organizations using AI tools, including cases where employees or contractors install them without approval, such as one customer that found an unapproved AI assistant accessing source code and being controlled through Telegram. The real-world concern is that attackers can move faster with AI while many companies are still slow to fix older known problems, so organizations may not see the easiest paths into their most important data and systems.

Summary
Tenable says AI adoption, especially unapproved “shadow AI,” is creating massive security exposure beyond traditional CVEs, with 457 million AI-related issues detected across 7,000+ organizations in 30 days. The key takeaway is that organizations need continuous AI-driven exposure management to find misconfigurations, exposed credentials, unmanaged dependencies, and attack paths before attackers exploit them.

Tenable says it detected 457 million AI-related security issues across more than 7,000 organizations during a 30-day period, averaging 62,000 exposures per organization. The article argues that approved and unapproved AI tools are creating a large wave of daily exposure, mostly tied to misconfigurations and unmanaged dependencies rather than standard CVEs. It says this makes it necessary for organizations to visualize, map, assess, and protect their environments through a comprehensive exposure management program.

The article says traditional vulnerability management remains important but is no longer enough, because many risks are not CVEs. It cites the 2026 Verizon Data Breach Investigations Report for findings that 31% of breaches start with an unpatched CVE, while Tenable telemetry says about 37% of findings are not CVEs but account for 63% of breach entry points. It also notes that organizations remain exposed to older issues including CVE-2024-21762 in Fortinet FortiOS, CVE-2021-44228 Log4Shell, and CVE-2017-0144 WannaCry.

Tenable highlights shadow AI as a major visibility problem, giving an example where a customer found 12 unapproved OpenClaw instances installed by a contractor on cloud workloads. The contractor had been given API keys and access to source code, and the OpenClaw instances were configured for remote management through Telegram. The article recommends AI-driven exposure management to continuously assess vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, excessive permissions, exposed secrets, and attack paths across IT, OT, AI, IoT, and cloud assets, with automated and agentic workflows used to help remediate critical exposures.

  • A green vulnerability remediation SLA dashboard can create false confidence because it may ignore non-CVE risks such as misconfigurations, stolen credentials, and exposed secrets that the article says account for most breach entry points.
  • The article highlights that patching performance is worsening at the same time AI is increasing attacker speed, with the median time-to-patch rising from 32 days to 43 days in the Verizon DBIR data it cites.
  • Shadow AI risk can enter through third parties, as shown by the example where a contractor installed unapproved OpenClaw instances on cloud workloads, used company API keys, accessed source code, and managed the tools remotely through Telegram.
  • Exposure counts alone may mislead remediation priorities because Tenable research cited in the article says each security finding creates an average of three attack paths, making the most important question which paths lead to critical assets.
Key Points
  • 01Tenable detected 457 million AI-related security issues across more than 7,000 organizations during a 30-day period, averaging 62,000 exposures per organization.
  • 02The article says AI-related security issues are primarily tied to misconfigurations and unmanaged dependencies rather than standard CVEs.
  • 03Tenable telemetry showed that some organizations remained exposed to older vulnerabilities, including CVE-2024-21762 in Fortinet FortiOS, CVE-2021-44228 Log4Shell, and CVE-2017-0144 WannaCry.
  • 04The article states that about 37% of Tenable findings are not CVEs but account for 63% of breach entry points.
  • 05The article argues that security teams should use AI-driven exposure management to continuously assess vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, excessive permissions, exposed secrets, and attack paths across IT, OT, AI, IoT, and cloud assets.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

The article provides notable Tenable telemetry about AI-related exposures and shadow AI, but it is primarily a broad trend and best-practice piece rather than a specific urgent incident, active exploitation report, or critical advisory. Its guidance is relevant for security professionals, but under the scoring rules generic trend pieces must remain low priority.

Reports
  • 2026 Verizon Data Breach Investigations Report
    Verizon
    The article cites the 2026 Verizon DBIR for statistics on breaches that start with unpatched CVEs, remediation rates for CISA KEV vulnerabilities, and median time-to-patch.
  • Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog
    Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency
    The article references CISA’s KEV catalog as the list of CVEs being actively exploited in the wild when discussing remediation rates cited from the Verizon DBIR.
02guide
How to evaluate Cloud Detection & Response (CDR) platforms and vendors
mitiga.io · 2026-06-24 21:31 openai / deepseek-v4-pro
TAGSawsgcphelios-aimicrosoft-365microsoft-azuremicrosoft-entra-idmitigaoktasalesforce
55
In plain words

This article is a checklist for companies choosing tools that help spot and respond to intruders across online business systems like cloud services, work apps, sign-in accounts, and AI systems. It matters because attackers often move through the gaps between these systems, especially by using valid accounts or permissions, and many tools may miss that bigger picture. The people affected are organizations that rely on these services and the teams trying to protect them, because the right tool can reduce how long an intruder goes unnoticed and how much damage they can cause.

Summary
The article provides a checklist for evaluating Cloud Detection and Response platforms, emphasizing that buyers should prioritize broad visibility across cloud, SaaS, identity, and AI systems, plus fast, context-aware response. The key test is whether a platform can measurably reduce detection time, response time, and blast radius in real cloud attacks.

The article presents a checklist for evaluating Cloud Detection and Response platforms and vendors based on real operational needs rather than feature lists. It argues that many enterprises have numerous security tools but still lack visibility that reflects how cloud, SaaS, identity, and AI systems behave. It says some CDR platforms are posture tools with alerts, while others are legacy detections wrapped in cloud branding.

The article emphasizes that a CDR platform should see across existing EDR telemetry, cloud control planes, SaaS, serverless systems, identity providers, workloads, storage, databases, APIs, networking logs, and configuration changes. It says attackers move through APIs, tokens, roles, permissions, and gaps between systems, so a platform that cannot correlate identity, SaaS, and cloud activity is only watching fragments. It also highlights AI-driven triage, cross-domain correlation, automatic attack path reconstruction, and machine-speed detection as ways to reduce noise and give analysts clarity.

The article says identity telemetry must be central because valid credentials can make traditional network assumptions irrelevant in cloud environments. It also says CDR platforms should support AI infrastructure security, near-real-time containment, integrations with SIEM, EDR/XDR, and SOAR, risk-aware prioritization, forensic readiness, and production-scale performance. Its final decision lens is whether the platform measurably reduces time-to-detect, time-to-respond, and blast radius across critical cloud, SaaS, and identity paths.

  • The article warns that some products labeled as CDR may actually be posture tools with alerts or legacy detection products rebranded for cloud, so buyers should pressure-test how they work in real operations.
  • The article frames agentless visibility as important because many critical areas, including SaaS, serverless, and identity providers, are places where traditional agents cannot run.
  • The article argues that response actions must be business-impact-aware because fast containment without context can cause outages, while slow containment can allow breaches.
  • The article treats forensic readiness as a selection criterion, including long-term data retention beyond native cloud limits and unified audit trails across cloud, identity, and workloads.
  • The article says CDR should act as an intelligence layer that strengthens SIEM, EDR/XDR, and SOAR tools rather than becoming another standalone dashboard.
Key Points
  • 01The article says Cloud Detection and Response platforms should be evaluated based on real operational needs rather than simple feature lists.
  • 02A CDR platform should provide visibility across cloud control planes, SaaS, identity providers, workloads, storage, databases, APIs, networking logs, and configuration changes.
  • 03The article emphasizes that AI-driven triage, cross-domain correlation, automatic attack path reconstruction, and machine-speed detection should reduce noise and give analysts clarity.
  • 04Identity telemetry is described as central to cloud detection because attackers with valid credentials can bypass traditional network-based assumptions.
  • 05The article says organizations should choose a CDR platform by whether it reduces time-to-detect, time-to-respond, and blast radius across critical cloud, SaaS, and identity paths.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is an evergreen buyer’s guide for evaluating Cloud Detection and Response platforms rather than coverage of a specific incident, vulnerability, exploit, or threat actor operation. It may be useful for teams assessing CDR vendors, but under the scoring rules generic best-practice guidance must remain low priority.

In plain words

The article is about healthcare workers using unapproved AI tools without their organizations officially tracking or approving them. These tools may help individuals save time, but healthcare organizations are affected because they can lead to data breaches and misuse of private data. The broader concern is that normal oversight is not keeping up, making this harder to control than earlier waves of unapproved technology use.

Summary
Healthcare workers are rapidly adopting unapproved “shadow AI” tools to save time, but this creates serious risks around data breaches, privacy misuse, and lack of organizational oversight. The key challenge is building governance that manages this usage rather than trying to stop it outright.

The article highlights a perspective piece on the spread of unapproved “off the books” AI tools in healthcare organizations. It describes these tools as “shadow AI” and says they are becoming widespread. The topic is presented by Errol Weiss, chief security officer of Health-ISAC.

The article says shadow AI can be difficult for organizations to track. It notes that these tools may save an individual valuable time, but they can also expose the organization to data breaches and misuse of private data. The piece frames the issue as a governance challenge for healthcare organizations.

The article identifies several themes covered in the linked Telehealth and Telecare Aware article. These include why governance keeps losing the race, why shadow AI is harder to contain than shadow IT ever was, and why organizations should “govern the current instead of damming it.” It also links to related Health-ISAC resources and news items on AI, healthcare, and security topics.

  • The article frames shadow AI as something healthcare organizations should manage through governance rather than simply try to block, using the idea of “govern the current instead of damming it.”
  • The article indicates that shadow AI presents a containment challenge beyond earlier “shadow IT” problems, suggesting existing approaches for unapproved technology may not be sufficient.
  • The article highlights a tradeoff that makes shadow AI difficult to address: the same tools that create privacy and breach risks may also save healthcare workers valuable time.
Key Points
  • 01The article discusses unapproved “off the books” AI tools, also called “shadow AI,” becoming widespread in healthcare organizations.
  • 02The article says these unapproved AI tools are difficult to track within healthcare organizations.
  • 03The article states that shadow AI may save individuals valuable time but can expose organizations to data breaches and misuse of private data.
  • 04The article is written by Errol Weiss, chief security officer of Health-ISAC.
  • 05The article highlights governance challenges, including why governance keeps losing the race and why shadow AI is harder to contain than shadow IT.
